entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 05 - Mobile Working with SAP EWM
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 05 – Mobile Working with SAP EWM

You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

Benefits of mobile working in the warehouse 

Mobile working in the warehouse makes it possible to record information on the spot, to react to exceptions in real time, to gain absolute inventory and process transparency and to create the foundation for comprehensive monitoring and analysis. 

The technologies of mobile working are diverse. They can be classic mobile devices, tablets or smartphones, pick-by-voice or pick-by-vision applications, gloves with scanners or even smart watches. But two things are certainly not part of it... paper and pen. 

Mobile working is also the prerequisite for a "system-guided working approach" which means that the warehouse management system EWM knows every open task and its priority in the warehouse and that the system also knows best which resource is the best for the task execution. This approach enables holistic optimization of warehouse operations.

Technology used for mobile working with SAP EWM

SAP EWM provides various technologies for mobile working. If we talk about mobile devices like handhelds, smartphones or tablets there are three strategic pillars for mobile working in EWM. 

Strategic pillars for mobile working with SAP EWM

Radio Frequency (RF) Framework

The RF framework in EWM is a comprehensive mobile environment which generates HTML pages from ABAP screens for the mobile devices. EWM delivers more than 130 mobile transactions for all processes in the warehouse, such as putaway, picking, inventory and much more, which can be used out-of-the-box.

SAP EWM RF Framework - system guided and mobile working in the warehouse

With the RF Framework customers can define via configuration if fields are visible or hidden and which fields are relevant for validation of a mobile working step for example a scan of the destination bin during putaway. It's also possible to include own fields or whole screens in standard EWM RF transactions or to build completely own mobile transactions. This is powered by the extension framework of the RF Framework!

Mobile working with the RF Framework provides highest performance on robust mobile devices that may not have the latest or strongest hardware. And performance is essential for the acceptance of these devices by the employees. Despite of SAP Fiori and native mobile Apps the RF framework is still one strategic pillar for mobile working with SAP EWM because of its large functional coverage, its high performance and its reliability for example if there are issues with network interruptions.

The RF frameworks has a built-in recovery concept. In case of an interruption the worker does not have to start the complete process again, but the RF framework ensures that the worker can continue exactly at the same step before the interruption happened. Recovery is an important aspect, because EWM RF standard transactions are designed as online dialogs, that means they need a connection to the backend to perform actions such as bin validation and they do not download any information to the devices. This concept enables real-time transparency and visibility in the EWM backend system. All mobile transactions in EWM are developed based on this recovery concept. For customer specific transactions this should of course be taken into account from the beginning. 

There are two options in SAP EWM to generate the HTML screens for the mobile devices: 

1) ITSmobile generates HTML screens during design time.
2) MobGUI generates HTML screens during runtime. 

RF Framework HTML screen generation with ITSmobile or MobGUI

 

HTML screen generation with ITSmobile
ITSmobile generates during design time HTML templates from the ABAP dynpros in the EWM backend. CSS stylesheets can be used for example to control the look of the generated HTML screens. There is also a visual editor which allows the graphical adaption of the screens. Therefore an intermediate step of XML generation takes place before the final HTML screens are generated. Layout and screen changes in the RF Framework in EWM always require re-generation of the HTML screens.

HTML screen generation with ITSmobile

 

HTML screen generation with MobGUI
MobGUI is an enhanced SAP GUI for HTML with features specifically for the radio frequency (RF) framework. The functionality in the back end is the same as in ITSmobile as it is the RF framework and the existing
RF transactions. The functionality in the front end is the same as SAP GUI for HTML with some optimization for
using on mobile devices, for example, hiding the header and footer line to allow more space for the RF screen.

Unlike with ITSmobile, the system doesn’t need to generate HTML templates from the ABAP dynpros. The screens are generated directly from the ABAP dynpros during runtime. This ensures you always use the latest screen version.

But without the generator and the HTML files when using ITSmobile, you can’t change a RF screen (such as CSS file or Visual Editor). For SAP GUI for HTML, you must use SAP Screen Personas for this. SAP Screen Personas is a No-Code/Low-Code tool to adjust screens to customer needs. This tool offers a WYSIWYG editor ("WYSIWYG - What you see is what you get") and a script engine for a huge range of adoption. 

No-Code/Low-Code screen personalization with SAP Screen Personas

There is also a How-to-Guide which describes the screen personalization for RF Screens with Screen Personas.

Important to mention: the generated RF Screens do not have a responsive design, as it is the case with native FIORI apps. The screens are generated according to the screen size of mobile devices.  However there are partner Add-Ons available which solve this requirement by generating HTML5 screens out of the templates of the RF Framework. You can find additional information about UI5 generator here. 

Extensibility concept of the RF Framework
SAP EWM actually covers all processes that "make sense" from a mobile perspective on mobile devices. Of course, the dialogs will not always meet the customer-specific requirements 100% - the perfect picking dialog for all industries & customers does not exist, due to the large variety of different industry requirements. But we are confident, that there should be a standard RF dialog that already comes close to the requirements - this dialogue can easily be adapted or extended via the RF framework without having to develop the entire dialog from scratch. In order to enable customers and partners to use and extend the RF Framework How-to-Guides are available - please check the following links: 

SAP EWM How-to-Guides - "Configure ITS Mobile" Cookbook
SAP EWM How-to-Guides - "RF Cookbook"

In a nutshell, the RF Framework is still one strategic pillar for mobile working with EWM. By the way: With the introduction of MobileGUI for the RF-Framework it is also available in the SAP S/4HANA public cloud warehouse management. In the private cloud environment it is of course usable via ITS-mobile generated HTMLs and via the mobile GUI.

There is also a RF picking transaction for pick-by-voice and the specific ITS (Internet Transaction Server) template generator 'Mobile Devices with Speech Input' which generates XHTML+Voice templates which can be consumed by third party voice recognition software on the pick by voice hardware to handle the voice output and voice input to the user. A cookbook how to build a voice-enabled transaction in SAP EWM is available. 

SAP Fiori

The SAP Fiori-Launchpad is our central end-user-interface also in SAP EWM on S/4HANA. And many native FIORI apps are already available for EWM today which are also usable on mobile devices like smartphones or tablets. For example the goods receipt and direct putaway using a tablet - or, for example, the warehouse KPIs, which can also be check briefly during a meeting using a smartphone.

Or the pick-by-cart app which can be used in EWM for multi-order picking – it was one of the first native FIORI apps in EWM.  

Fiori Apps in SAP EWM - examples for physical inventory and picking with cart

The full list of available Fiori Apps for SAP EWM is available in the SAP Fiori App Library.

Mobile Apps based on APIs

In addition there is the possibility to develop own custom mobile applications which can then run on smartphones or even on smart watches for example a mobile picking dialog. For this SAP EWM offers numerous well-documented remote APIs. You can find them in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, our central landing page for extensions & innovations.

Mobile Apps for SAP EWM based on APIs

This enables customers to develop their own mobile UI5 apps or even native iOS or Android Apps. With SAP Build Apps SAP offers also No-Code-Low-Code Tool on the SAP Business Technology Platform for the app development. All BTP services can be explored in the SAP Discovery Center. 

SAP provides such a native App based on APIs for the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Warehouse Management: The Warehouse Operator App which can downloaded from the Apple Store for example. 

Warehouse Operator App for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Warehouse Management

 

There is also a comprehensive offering of partner add-ons for mobile working with SAP EWM. Many partners for example have already build mobile Apps based on APIs and offer their mobile Apps as packaged solutions either in the SAP Store or as accelerators for SAP EWM implementation projects. But there are also Apps in the SAP Store to generate UI5s directly out of the RF Framework screens as well as apps for pick-by-vision scenarios. The SAP store is the central entry point if you are looking for partner add-ons for mobile working with SAP EWM.

Conclusion

SAP EWM provides customers with various options for mobile working. With the RF Framework, SAP EWM offers a ready-to-use mobile environment that supports almost all processes in the warehouse. For customers who also want to use UI5 on mobile devices in the warehouse, SAP EWM pursues a clear API strategy and offers the option of developing own mobile apps with the tools of the SAP Business Technology Platform.

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 17 - Batch management with SAP EWM!
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 17 – Batch management with SAP EWM.

You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

Batch Management is relevant for every company! 

Almost all industries and sectors - and not only producing companies – have the requirement to manage and track homogeneous partial quantities of materials or articles across the entire supply chain. This can be a specific delivery quantity of an article in retail, a production batch in food production, a specific quantity of screws delivered by a supplier in discrete industry which are used as components for production or any kind of in chemistry or pharma industry. 

SAP EWM solves this requirement with the help of the SAP Batch, which is seamlessly integrated into all SAP EWM processes and enables seamless tracking of these partial quantities across the supply chain.

The SAP Batch - a short introduction

In an SAP ERP system, material quantities and stock values are usually managed on storage location level. For many reasons, it could be that inventory management at material level is not sufficient. For example if different production batches of a product need to be managed or if delivery lots or production lots from suppliers need to be managed clearly separated from each other in the system. Differentiating characteristics are, for example, the country of origin, the production date, the supplier batch, the best-before date and many more.

The batch is an SAP ERP master data!

And that’s where the SAP Batch comes into the game. In order to use batches for a material the Batch Management Requirement Indicator must be set in the SAP ERP material master. The batch management requirement indicator makes it mandatory for a material to capture a batch for each partial quantity of the material.

The batch itself is also an SAP ERP master data. It consists of a batch master record and an optional classification. The batch master record already contains many standard fields that are logistically relevant and can be used in EWM. The country of origin, the manufacturing and expiry date, the supplier batch and also the batch status are such standard fields of the batch master for example. The batch is part of the inventory management, both in SAP Inventory Management and in EWM and it is a so-called stock separation criteria. 

The SAP Batch

If these standard fields are not sufficient, a batch can also be classified. Classification means that any characteristics can be defined to further differentiate a batch and make it unique. In this way, any stock-dividing criteria can be included in a batch.

It is not necessary to always assign values to every characteristic of a batch. And as already mentioned, the classification is optional, and customers can also work only with the batch master record. This means that customers can use the batch to define basically any stock-dividing criteria in the SAP standard!

 The Levels of Batch Management or "How unique is batch?"

Each partial quantity of the material thus becomes uniquely identifiable in the ERP on storage location level and in SAP EWM on storage bin or handling unit level. The uniqueness of a batch in SAP ERP can be defined on different levels.

1) A batch can be unique at SAP client level for example that means the batch ID will only exist once in the whole technical SAP client.

2) Another possibility is the uniqueness on material level that means a batch ID can exist only once per material. However, different materials can have batches with the same batch ID.

3) And the third level of uniqueness is the plant level that means a batch for a material is only unique within a plant and batch IDs in different plants can be completely different objects.

The good news is that SAP EWM can work with all these levels of ERP batch management. However, the batch at the plant level has limitations and is usually not used when batches are newly implemented.

The role of batches in SAP EWM

Since the batch is an ERP master data object, an embedded EWM in S/4HANA accesses this object directly. In decentralized EWM deployments, the batches, the classification and changes of batches are distributed from SAP ERP to EWM. Up to release EWM 9.5, the Core Interface (CIF) was used for this master data distribution. For the decentralized EWM on S/4HANA systems the BATMAS Idoc and also the classification IDOCs in ERP are used because the data model of the batch and the classification in the decentralized EWM on S/4HANA is the same data model as in SAP ERP.

And of course, batch creation is also possible within SAP EWM for example during EWM inbound delivery processing or via the dedicated app "Create Batch". In this use case EWM sends the batch including its characteristics back to the SAP ERP system after creation or changes in EWM.

Batches in EWM inbound processing
In SAP EWM batches can be created either manually or as part of the goods receipt, if they are not already transferred from ERP for example in an advanced shipping notification. In the inbound delivery, batches can be created manually or also in the background - with internal numbering or by entering an external batch ID.

Batch creation in EWM inbound processing

 

Standard characteristics such as the country of origin, the expiry date or the supplier batch can be automatically transferred to the batch from the preceding documents.  Customer-specific characteristics can be automatically filled by BAdI implementations which can transfer these values into the batch in the background as well. In addition, a check of the minimum remaining shelf life in goods receipt can be activated also, for example to prevent the receiving of expired materials or of materials with too short a remaining life.

Storage of batch managed stock in the EWM warehouse
By configuration, customers can also define in EWM whether different batches are allowed within a Handling Unit or in a storage bin, or whether only one batch is allowed per Handling Unit or per storage bin. This of course affects the packing and putaway processes within EWM.

EWM also makes it possible to manage the available stock in a storage bin for a batch-managed material in a batch-neutral way and to store the batch information only in the physical stock. This gives the picker the freedom to choose one of the available batches in case of mixed batch stock in a storage bin without the need to search for a certain predefined batch. 

Examples of EWM physical stock for batch managed materials

Batches in EWM outbound processing
This leads us directly to the goods issue process and the stock removal strategies for batches. EWM can either receive a specific batch from the ERP in the outbound delivery for batch-managed materials, or it can receive only certain selection criteria or even no batch specification at all. The given information is then taken into account in the SAP EWM stock removal strategy. In this way, customers can control, for example, that only goods that meet certain minimum remaining shelf lives are picked and shipped for certain customers.

EWM stock removal strategies based on predefined batch characteristics

Immediately after picking, EWM then sends the picked batches back to the SAP ERP outbound delivery and therefore significantly earlier than other 3rd party WMS systems with the goods issue posting.

In addition batches are also supported in SAP EWM production logistics which enables a seamless traceability from the goods receipt of components through staging, consumption and goods receipt of finished goods. This traceability is also ensured across system boundaries for example in the interaction with an MES system as we also discussed in our podcast about SAP Digital Manufacturing Integration.

Batch Status Management
Another important aspect is batch status management. If problems arise related to a batch for example quality issues or similar, then customers sometimes have to block a batch immediately for any goods movements to prevent further deliveries to customers. And this is exactly what batch status management supports. The batch status is a characteristic of the batch, which can have two status values: unrestricted and restricted. 

Configuration of restricted goods movements in warehouse for batches which are not in free use

 And exactly in the moment the batch status is set to restricted in one system it is also set in all other connected systems accordingly also for example in decentralized EWM systems. And based on EWM configuration it is possible to control which goods movements are then still allowed for a batch within the warehouse. For example, picking could be still possible but goods issue postings should be prevented.

Customers therefore only need to set one status centrally, for example in the SAP ERP system, to prevent batches with issues from being delivered further by logistics in any EWM managed warehouse. 

Split Valuation - Use batches to manage stock with different stock value 
The batch in EWM is also used when customers use the split valuation of materials in SAP ERP since split valuation basically has the same requirement as normal batches.

There are partial quantities of a product in stock which have to be distinguished from other partial quantities, in this case because of their stock value. SAP ERP uses split valuation for this purpose. SAP EWM uses the batch for materials which are not batch managed to manage differently valuated stock – the batch id corresponds in this case to the ERP valuation type.

For materials which are already batch managed the valuation type is directly linked to the batch master record.

Segmentation - Use batches to manage stock with for different markets 
Another use case of more granular stock differentiation than just on material level is demand and supply segmentation in SAP ERP, which is particular used in the retail and fashion industry.

These processes again have the requirement to divide the total stock of a material into multiple partial quantities with different characteristics. These characteristics in the segmentation can be, for example, the distribution channels and/or the product quality. 

Example:
A fashion company wants to control the stock of a certain product in a way that partial quantities are reserved for certain distribution channels like online stores and in-stores. And the company wants to procure and distribute the quantities for the different channels. This can be achieved with segmentation in SAP ERP.
In the procurement and in inventory management so called stock segments are used and in the distribution process so called requirement segments.

A segment is the combination of characteristics that makes such a partial stock quantity unique, for example stock of a certain material for the e-commerce channel. Also the combination of several characteristics is possible for example quality A of a material for the e-commerce channel.

In SAP S/4HANA segmentation requires batch managed materials and for each stock segment a batch is defined which has the corresponding stock segment assigned as a characteristic. That means a batch represents the stock of a certain combination for example the quality A of a material for the e-commerce channel.

Stock segmentation with SAP EWM

In procurement, companies can then build up stock for certain segmentations. In EWM, the corresponding stock is built up with the batch representing the stock segment and in the EWM inbound delivery the stock segment is also visible at delivery item level.

And in the outbound process, the ERP can then send a requirement segment in the outbound delivery item, which must then be picked by EWM. That means EWM only selects stock for picking with the corresponding stock segment. If there is no specification of a requirement segment for an outbound delivery item, EWM will perform a batch determination based on the segmentation strategy stored in the material master.

Segmented products are of course also supported in other EWM processes such as returns processes and also in production logistics.

Internal warehouse processes with batch involvement 
Stock Consolidation 
EWM stock consolidation can consolidate stocks in the warehouse even at batch characteristic level in order to better utilize warehouse capacity. This may result in mixed-batch stocks in one bin, but the different stocks must be identical in one or more batch characteristics from a logistical point of view, such as the expiration date or almost identical for example the expiration date of all consolidated batches in one bin is within a specified time period.

Stock consolidation in SAP EWM based on batch characteristica

 Recurring QM Inspections 
SAP EWM also supports recurring inspections for batch managed materials. In this case, the batch has the next inspection date as a characteristic. A report in EWM periodically checks batches based on this characteristic and, if necessary, creates inspection lots for quality inspection and posts the stock to quality stock. A successful QM inspection then determines the next inspection date, updates the batch characteristics with the new date and releases the stock again.

Recurring inspection with SAP EWM

 

Documentary Batch
In summary, it is fair to say that the SAP batch in EWM enables more complex processes. If EWM customers do not want to introduce inventory-managed batches but still want to achieve a certain traceability, the documentary batch is an option. The documentary batch is not master data but it is captured in goods receipt and in the goods issue process when picking is confirmed. The documentary batch is then stored in the batch field of the inbound and outbound delivery item for documentation reasons on document item level. This enables logistics to do a lean where-used approach via the documentary batch ID. But the documentary batch does support classification, batch status management and so on.

Batch Traceability 

Full transparency is only possible with the inventory managed SAP Batch!

Batch Information Cockpit
Customers receive the full "batch where-used list" only with the inventory-managed SAP batch. All material movements of a batch within the SAP ERP system can be analyzed with the help of the Batch Information Cockpit and a complete traceability from goods receipt through production and shipping processes is possible at the push of a button.

SAP Global Batch Traceability (GBT) 
For customers who have more advanced requirements with regards to batch traceability, the SAP Global Batch Traceability solution – or SAP GBT - can be a very useful complementary solution. It allows integrate various SAP and non-SAP systems that collect batch information and it enables company-wide batch traceability. 

SAP EWM can also deliver information to SAP Global Batch Traceability directly which not only contains the batch but also load carrier information that means Handling Unit data. This enables SAP GBT to do a batch tracing at pallet or load carrier level and makes it possible in exceptional cases to only recall a few pallets instead of an entire production batch – a huge benefit.

SAP Global Batch Traceability provides special reports and visualizations for this purpose, which can be used to analyze and trace the use of batches along the value chain on an ad-hoc basis to identify in a case of a problem critical deliveries in seconds. The solution is often used by chemical, pharmaceutical and consumer goods companies, but there are also use-cases in discrete manufacturing or in the automotive sector.

SAP Global Batch Traceability uses SAP EWM Handling Unit information

 SAP Business Network Material Traceability

SAP Business Network Material Traceability is a solution that also re-uses batch information from S/4HANA for traceability. Compared to the batch information cockpit in ERP and SAP GBT, this solution focuses on the cross-company traceability of product batches across all stages of a supply chain - from the supplier of the supplier to the end customer. In other words - External Tracking. 
The solution also works with batch information from the S/4HANA system, which can also be created in EWM.

SAP Busines Network Material Traceability

 Conclusion

The SAP Batch is a very powerful object that can solve many requirements related to product and batch traceability. Therefore it should be considered in any SAP S/4HANA implementation because it is the answer of SAP when it comes to traceability of stock on more granular level than the material. At first glance, it could create a little more recording effort in the warehouse, since it has to be additionally recorded or scanned in many processes. But for the whole company however this information is very valuable and often even required or mandatory. SAP EWM offers many possibilities to keep the effort of batch recording in the warehouse as little as possible for example by just scanning EAN128 barcodes. And there for complex processes also with batch managed materials can be highly automated with SAP EWM.

Additional Information
Batch Management with SAP - Product Documentation
How-to-Guide for the SAP EWM - SAP GBT Integration
SAP Global Batch Traceability
SAP Business Network Material Traceability
SAP Batch Information Cockpit

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: Brief Overview Stock-Specific Unit of Measure Overview In SAP EWM
entry.content:
Hi All 

Today I am writing blog for concept of SUoM in SAP EWM

Stock-Specific Unit of Measure Overview and usage

 The stock-specific unit of measure (SUoM) is a second unit of measure beside the base unit of measure, which is available in the background in the stock data and in logistic-relevant documents such as inbound and outbound deliveries and warehouse tasks. This UoM covers the data capturing during the inbound process, and the use of it in the internal and outbound processes. In the outbound process, the ERP outbound delivery can be updated with the physically picked UoM, based on the SUoM. It is deployable to the customer through a delivery note and/or invoice, so that the documents reflect the physical goods.

Activate the business function EWM SCM_EWM_FND

Effects on Master Data

If you use the stock-specific unit of measure (UoM) functionality in Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), the alternative UoM of your stock gets an important stock attribute, as it reflects the physical packaging of your products. If you change the UoM quantity conversion factors in the product master, it affects this attribute.

Note-If you would like to define new packaging sizes for your product, you should introduce new UoMs instead, so that existing stock is not affected.

Preferred Unit of Measure

The preferred unit of measure (UoM) is the packaging size in which you usually store a product in your warehouse. You can maintain the preferred UoM in the material master at a global level and in the product master at a warehouse level via transaction code /SCWM/MAT1. The system uses the global preferred UoM in warehouse processes unless you maintain a warehouse-specific one.

The Preferred UoM is used as a proposal in below Warehouse Processes

Inbound Process

Outbound Process

Physical Inventory Process

Note-You can also use the warehouse management monitor node Stock Not In Pref. UoM to identify stock which is not in the preferred UoM. In the standard SAP monitor structure you can find this node under Stock and Bin.

SUoM in Inbound Processes

 You can use stock-specific unit of measure (SUoM) functionality in inbound processes in a variety of ways, including inbound delivery, packing, quality inspection, deconsolidation and putaway.

SUoM in Outbound Processes

You can use stock-specific unit of measure (SUoM) functionality in outbound processes in a variety of ways, including picking and outbound delivery.

 

SUoM in Physical Inventory

 You can use stock-specific unit of measure (SUoM) functionality in physical inventory processes in a variety of ways.

SUoM in Cross Processes

You can use stock-specific unit of measure (SUoM) functionality in cross processes in a variety of ways, including in opportunistic cross-docking, in the warehouse management monitor, and in conjunction with radio frequency functions. You cannot use it in conjunction with catch weight functions.

 

Conclusion- This blog will you take through concept of SUOM. It’s has several benefits by implementing it.

Please see this  blog - https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blogs-by-members/preferred-alternative-uom-for-warehouse-operation-in-ewm/ba-p/13679453

entry.title: Warehouse Requests in SAP S/4 Hana Embedded EWM
entry.content:
Hi All 

I am writing blogs about warehouse request in sap s/4 hana embedded EWM.

What is Warehouse Requests ?

The warehouse request is a document  that enables the processing of warehouse activities for a specific product. Warehouse requests are planned documents for warehouse stock movements within and outside the warehouse. They form the basis for creation of warehouse tasks, which are assigned to warehouse workers for stock movement completion. Some of the warehouse requests that can be created in embedded EWM are outbound delivery orders, inbound delivery orders, posting change documents, and so on. In the following sections, we explain the business context in which the physical inventory process can be used in real-world scenarios and how to carry these processes out in the system.

SAP S/4HANA delivery or posting change requests become warehouse requests in embedded EWM. A warehouse request is the planning document for all internal movements in embedded EWM and is used only in embedded EWM. A warehouse request is made up of a document header and document type. The document header contains general data relevant to the entire request and the entire document; the document item contains data specific to each item in the delivery document, with the option to create follow-up documents and take follow-up actions for each item, independent of the delivery document header

The Warehouse Activities for a Product are as below

  • Picking
  • Putaway
  • Posting change
  • Stock transfer (within warehouse)
  • Scrapping

Type of warehouse requests

  • Inbound delivery
  • Outbound delivery order
  • Posting change
  • Internal stock transfer

Configuration

Service profiles are used in embedded EWM to define how documents are processed and to define the business features of the documents. These profiles are associated with the document type and item type in embedded EWM and control their features and behavior.

.There are two types of service profiles in embedded EWM.

 

System profile

System profiles are included in the scope of the standard embedded EWM system and can’t be modified. Internally, system profiles are linked to both document category profiles related to header settings and to item categories for item profile settings.

 

Customer profile

Customer profiles are required to manage individual business processes and can be modified based on business requirements. SAP has provided a set of predefined customer profiles, each of which is linked to a corresponding system profile. The customer profiles are assigned to document types and item types based on the specific business process for which they are used.

The various service profiles available in embedded EWM are explained in the following  sections

 

Process Code Profile

Process codes are used in warehouse requests to perform quantity adjustments at the item level. A process code profile determines which process codes are available for use in a warehouse request and is assigned at the item type level. SAP provides predefined process code profiles that are assigned to the corresponding system profiles. If you want to use different process codes for a business process, you can create a new process code profile and assign it to the system profile.

You can also create new process codes for a business process

IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Process Codes • Define Process Codes

 

 

To create a new process code profile or make changes to an existing one, navigate to IMG • Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Process Codes • Maintain Process Code Profiles

 

 

 

Status Profile

A status profile is used to control the status of a warehouse request to allow or prohibit processing from certain business transactions. You can use status management to set a status in a document for information purposes only or to change a document status after transaction execution.

 

To define a status profile, navigate to IMG • Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Process Codes • Define Status Profile

 

 

 

Quantity Offsetting Profile

Define the quantity offsetting profile  IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery Warehouse Requests • Quantity Offsetting • Define Quantity Offsetting Profiles

 

 

 

Text Profile

Use text types to copy text in the SAP S/4HANA delivery to the warehouse request in embedded EWM. You can also use text types to add text directly to the embedded EWM delivery. A text profile is used to group together text types for a delivery header or item. You can create text types for the delivery header and delivery item levels. Text types at the delivery header level include shipping instructions, bills of lading, and so on.

 

To define the text profiles for delivery documents via IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Text Management • Define Text Profiles with Access Sequence

 

 

 

Field Control Profile

A field control is used to determine which fields are displayed as display-only or modifiable fields during delivery processing. A field control profile contains all allowed fields and their attributes for a document or item type.

define a field control profile via IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery Warehouse Requests • Field Control • Define Field Control Profiles

 

 

 

 

 

 

Incompleteness Check Profile

Embedded EWM allows the system to perform an incompleteness check on a docu[1]ment to ensure that all required fields are filled before a document can be processed. These mandatory field names are mapped to a logical field name and contained in an incompleteness profile, which is assigned to a delivery document type for header fields and a delivery item type for item-related fields.

Defined IMG • Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Incompleteness Check • Define Incompleteness Profile

 

 

 

Action Profile

Embedded EWM uses the PPF for message/output control in delivery processing. All actions in a warehouse request document are generated by the framework based on action definitions and processed by the system. These actions can be print commands, email alerts, and so on. The actions are stored in an action profile assigned to a document type in EWM.

 

Set a PPF action profile via IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Actions • Change Actions and Conditions • Define Action Profiles and Actions.

 

 

 

Partner Profile

A delivery document in embedded EWM contains partner information required for further processing of the warehouse request. For example, the ship-to party is a man[1]datory business partner required in an outbound delivery order to determine the ship-to address for the delivery items. Partner processing provides the ability to maintain partner information at the delivery header or item level.

Define partner roles IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Partner Processing • Define Partner Roles

 

 

 

To create or change customer partner profiles IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Partner Processing • Define Partner Profiles

 

 

Reference Document Profile

 

A reference document profile allows you to add one or more preceding document references in embedded EWM. Each document is categorized using a reference document category code.

 IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Reference Documents • Define Reference Document Categories

 

Once the reference document categories are defined, they can be assigned to a reference document profile

IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Reference Documents • Define Reference Document Types Profiles

 

 

Date Profile

In embedded EWM, it’s possible to add new date types in warehouse request delivery documents to capture the date and time of various system processes for the document. SAP has provided predefined date types, but you can add new date/time types.

IMG-Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Date/Times • Define Date Types

 

 

The new date/time types are defined, they can be bundled into date profiles

 IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Reference Documents • Define Date Profile

 

 

Process Profile

Embedded EWM uses process profiles for delivery headers and items, which control the processes allowed in a delivery document. Embedded EWM supports separate process profiles for delivery headers and items.

To define a process profile for a delivery header via IMG • Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery Warehouse Requests • Process Profile • Define Process Profile for Delivery Header

 

 

 

You can create process profiles for a document item

 IMG• Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Process Profile • Define Process Profile for Delivery Item

 

To create a number range for warehouse requests, navigate to IMG • Extended Warehouse Management • Cross-Process Settings • Delivery-Warehouse Requests • Number Ranges

 

Using Inactive and Active Document Versions

EWM can accept or reject change messages for inbound deliveries from your enterprise management system, for example SAP S/4HANA or SAP ERP. EWM uses inactive and active document versions for this. If warehouse activities (follow-on activities) have already begun for inbound deliveries, EWM rejects change messages from the enterprise management system. For example, EWM has already created a warehouse task (WT) for an inbound delivery. In doing so, EWM has also already set the status type Warehouse Activities to the status value Partially Completed. In the case of outbound deliveries, EWM can only accept a reduction of the quantity from the enterprise management system. If warehouse activities (follow-on activities) have already begun for inbound deliveries, EWM rejects the message from the enterprise management system.

Prerequisites

The document type of your request document contains an action profile with an action for activating the request document, for example /SCDL/IDR or /SCDL/ODR.

Note - If you do not want to activate your request documents immediately, either create your own action profile or do not assign an action profile to your request document type.

 

Please provide your feed back to improve it 

Reference https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/9832125c23154a179bfa1784cdc9577a/31d75d84edc2489b8124b0afaf3c374d.html

entry.title: Introducing Warehousing Concept in SAP S/4 HANA Supply Chain Execution
entry.content:
Hi All

I am explaining warehousing in Supply Chain Execution in SAP S/4 Hana

Brief Overview to SAP S/4 Hana and Features

With SAP S/4HANA, SAP is providing a product that represents the next generation of business applications. Simple enterprise software that is designed to help you run simple in the digital economy. SAP S/4HANA supports

Customer in dealing with challenges like integrating business data with data outside of our company and supporting our business processes with artificial intelligence (or AI). The SAP S/4HANA family is completely built on the in-memory platform SAP HANA. Using the potential of the SAP HANA platform, SAP S/4HANA is designed for customer digital business. It provides an instant insight into where your business currently stands. It does this by combining a single source of truth with dynamic planning and analysis capabilities and real-time process support.

With the SAP Fiori user experience and a less complex data model, SAP S/4HANA is designed to run simple. In parallel, it reduces the data footprint of your company.

 The SAP S/4HANA system is also already connected to business networks and company internal collaboration networks like SAP Build Work Zone. And it also prepares us for supporting your processes with SAP Business AI. All of these aspects protect your investment into SAP software by facilitating the next generation of business applications.

SAP S/4 Hana comes with following Product Versions

SAP S/4 Hana on premises

SAP S/4HANA On-Premises managed by SAP (HEC)

SAP S/4 Hana Public Edition

SAP S/4 Hana Private Edition

 

SAP S/4HANA contains the below elements

SAP S/4HANA Enterprise Management.

SAP S/4HANA Line-of-Business Products.

SAP S/4HANA Line-of-Business (LoB) Products for specific industries.

SAP S/4HANA Compatibility Packs.

Supply Chain Management Capability in S/4 Hana

 

SAP S/4HANA enables companies to integrate and visualize all supply chain processes from supplier to customer. Intelligence can also be embedded in these processes. Digital supply chain and mission-critical business processes are supported across the enterprise with one single source of live information such as

  • Reduce Days of Inventory
  • Reduction in Revenue Loss Due to Fulfilment Issues
  • Reduced Overall Supply Chain Planning Costs
  • Improved Supply Chain Planning, increasing Productivity
  • Reduced TCO Through Landscape Simplification

Digital Supply Chain Execution

The core functionality provided by SAP S/4HANA can be extended with other (sometimes Cloud based) SAP products, to support the complete logistics network of a company.

Additional SAP products that support the complete supply chain

SAP Global Batch Traceability

SAP AI Core

SAP Yard Logistics

SAP User Experience Strategy

The use of mobile devices (smart-phones and tablets) to perform certain work tasks, is no longer reserved to a special group of experts. Ease of use is a very important characteristic for the end user of such a device. The focus of the user interface (UI) of the device is no longer maximum functionality, but rather a comfortable user experience and thus a focus on the consumer of the device. With this in mind, the business software running behind the device must be adapted and transformed so that it supports the user interface (used within the business roles of an enterprise) to a maximum. In practice this means that SAP S/4HANA and SAP Fiori as its user interface go hand-in-hand, and should be developed hand-in-hand.

Different User Types — Different Requirements

SAP Fiori

SAP Fiori is a collection of apps, which is created based on a specific set of guidelines and rules and that can be started using the SAP Fiori launchpad.

Available Warehouse Management Solutions in SAP S/4 Hana

Warehouse Management LE-WM

The classic SAP ERP solution for warehouse management LE-WM is part of SAP S/4HANA through a Compatibility Package (CP). This provides customers with a limited use right which expires on December 31, 2027. The inclusion of LE-WM in SAP S/4HANA helps customers migrating from SAP ERP to SAP S/4HANA to start using some of the advanced features of SAP S/4HANA without having to also immediately migrate their warehouse management solution at the same time. For existing customers that do not require complex warehouse processing in their SAP S/4HANA system, with release 1909 of SAP S/4HANA a basic warehouse management solution has been introduced in SAP S/4HANA called Stock Room Management.

Stock Room Management

The main reason for adding Stock Room Management to SAP S/4HANA is to give existing customers of LE-WM an opportunity to keep their existing LE-WM warehouses after 2027 that do not benefit immediately from moving to (embedded) SAP EWM. Stock Room Management is relevant for small warehouses with manual operations that manage stocks on storage bin level. this deployment allows customers to upgrade to SAP S/4HANA without performing a full-fledged WM transformation.

LE-WM features that are not supported in Stock Room Management include

  • Task and Resource Management (WM-TRM)
  • Warehouse Control Unit interface (WM-LSR)
  • Value Added Service (WM-VAS)
  • Yard Management (WM-YM)
  • Cross-Docking (WM-CD)
  • Wave Management (WM-TFM-CP)
  • Decentralized WM (WM-DWM)

SAP Extended Warehouse Management (SAP EWM)

SAP EWM first emerged as a successor to SAP Logistics Execution Warehouse Management (LE-WM), with added advantages and advanced features such as material flow system (MFS) and Labor Management. The earliest versions of SAP EWM were only available as separate standalone applications until 2016 when SAP embedded the EWM functionality in the  SAP S/4 Hana Suite. SAP EWM can be installed as standalone or be connected to an SAP S/4HANA system.

The solution can be deployed as Embedded or Decentral. In both case for On Premise or running SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private Edition.

Embedded EWM in SAP S/4HANA

Embedded SAP EWM is also available. Embedded means that no separate system is required and therefore SAP EWM can be used as a direct replacement for LE-WM. EWM is in such a set-up completely embedded within the SAP S/4HANA system. By embedding EWM on SAP S/4HANA, clients could take advantage of the SAP HANA in-memory database  and execute high-speed transactions for their warehousing operations. With each new version of SAP S/4HANA, more functionality was added to embedded EWM, including an ever-growing list of innovative SAP Fiori apps and a stripped-down version called stock room management.

The license type differentiation for Basic or  Advanced is only applicable for embedded deployment cases.

To Choose Basic or Advanced Please customized under IMG-EWM-Master Data -Acknowledge Use of Advance Function

Basic Extended Warehouse Management

Basic license type, customers are able to perform the main process end to end such as, Inbound, Outbound, Production Integration, Quality management integration, etc. Below you can find all the process included on basic license:


Inventory Management 

Inbound Processing 

Outbound Processing 

Internal Warehouse Movements

Physical Inventory

Reporting (Warehouse Management Monitor, Analytics)

Resource Management

Quality Management

Production Integration

Integration with Transportation Management (ASR)

IDOC Integration to third-party MFS systems (SAP Note 2873423)

Advanced Extended Warehouse Management

 The advanced license might be required in case of customer would use some features enclosed of the main process, for example, the outbound process designed is required to use Wave management or, the production integration is required to use JIT - Just in Time. It means that the Advanced license type is required in case of use some specific SAP EWM features, such as

Material Flow System (MFS)

Wave Management

Transportation units

Yard Management

Labor Management

Cross Docking

Value Added Services (VAS)

Warehouse Billing

Kitting

Dock Appointment Scheduling (DAS)

Slotting

Cartonization planning

Distribution Equipment

JIT

WIP Tracking

Warehouse Zones for advanced interleaving

License Management for SAP EWM Embedded

 The list of the extended objects for SAP EWM embedded is technically found on table /SCWM/EXT_OBJ. SAP Product Solution Team provide 2 reports for customers perform in their system in order to know which objects is being used on their warehouse system with usage detail

  • Report /SCWM/RP_COMPLIANCE_CHECK is available since S/4HANA 2016 (and higher) and provides the information if the customizing or the licensing  is compliance or non-compliance for each warehouse system.
  • Report /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_SELF_AUDIT is available since S/4HANA 2022 (and higher) and provides by warehouse number a view by month of usage of Basic and Advanced features by total number of items per month, number of average items per day and active working days, compliance or non-compliance from the customizing perspective

 Decentralized EWM in SAP S/4HANA

After SAP launched embedded EWM in SAP S/4HANA 1610, there were a lot of customers who realized that they wanted to use their existing systems but also embedded EWM features in another SAP S/4HANA physical server. The reasoning was this would ensure higher performance levels and warehouse business process continuity in case of any downtime. To cater to such a requirement, SAP introduced a new deployment option known as decentralized EWM in SAP S/4HANA 1809, FPS 01. Prior to this version, embedded EWM used to share the same master data objects which were loaded as part of the same SAP S/4HANA instance and could not interact with the master data of other SAP ERP  or SAP S/4HANA instances.  With the new decentralized deployment, clients can take advantage of current and new innovations in embedded EWM but keep on using separate SAP ERP or SAP S/4HANA core systems.

Note- The Decentralize deployment always requires the advanced license type

Types of SAP EWM Warehouses

 

Types of warehouses and their main tasks could be

Production Warehouse

  • Raw-materials handling
  • Integration of quality management
  • Production supply
  • Receipts from production
  • Materials staging and consumption

Distribution Center

  • Complex process and high volume
  • High degree of automation
  • Wave management
  • Slotting and rearrangement
  • Replenishment

Service Parts Warehouse 

  • Low to very high complexity
  • Kitting and value-added services
  • High volatility
  • Serial numbering
  • Heterogeneous product ranges

Cross-Dock or Transit Warehouse 

  • Regional hubs, gateways, and container freight stations
  • Air and ocean freight handling
  • Unit-load devices and containers handling
  • Receive from and sent to airport or seaport (drayage)

Conclusion- It will give a brief idea about the products and warehouse Management which will help to utilize knowledge to choose correct option for warehouse in Supply chain Management.

Reference https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blogs-by-sap/understanding-license-type-basic-vs-advanced-for-sap-extended-warehouse/ba-p/13580547

SAP Help 

Please provide the feed back to improve it

entry.title: Quality inspection in SAP EWM using RFUI
entry.content:
Imagine the following scenario: an end-user is performing stock removals using RF gun with SAP EWM, and notice a possible damaged stock in some near storage bin. The user wants to continue the picking for operational performance reasons, following the determined picking route, but also needs to somehow register the situation and trigger a quality inspection process.

In this blog post, I will explain a standard business process to satisfy this requirement, triggering a QM process for a possibly damaged stock, making it unavailable for picking Warehouse Task creation until a usage decision is made.

Additionally, I will also cover specific Decentral EWM Quality Management inspection processes which differentiate very significantly from Embedded EWM capabilities from RFUI perspective. 

1. Introduction

Both Embedded and Decentral deployments of SAP EWM offer the possibility of using ERP QM engine for inspections, but Decentral option also offers the possibility of using EWM QIE (Quality Inspection Engine) alone, which may run independently from SAP ERP. EWM QIE offers different capabilities when compared to S/4HANA QM.

The focus of this blog is the quality inspection process using RFUI, and not the QM configuration itself. I recommend reading the reference documentation at the end of this blog for an explanation of Quality Management within SAP EWM.

The biggest difference from RF perspective is that Embedded EWM only allows the creation of the inspection document from RF (either product or HU), while Decentral EWM allows the recording of inspection decisions directly in RF. This is explained in the SAP Note 2861813 - "Quality Management (QM) in embedded EWM S/4HANA system".

2. Required Configuration

If you are running Decentral EWM in SAP S/4HANA 2021 or higher, before you start any configuration, make sure that the manual steps of the SAP Note 3282994 - "Adjust visibility of RF-QM transactions" are followed. The visibility of the logical transactions QMCOUM, QMCOUN, QMDHAS, QMDHHU, QMDHTU, QMDHVE, QMHUAS, QMHUCL, QMHUDV, QMHUHU, QMHUTU, QMHUVE, QMLOGN, QMPD**, QMPDHU, QMPDID, QMPDPR, QMPDSB and QMPDSD must be set to 'F' as per screenshot below:

As explained above, the focus of this blog is not QM configuration, but the usage of RFUI transaction to run QM processes (e.g. start of an inspection process using Radio Frequency). To begin with, we need to activate the required logical transactions using transaction /SCWM/RFMENU for our presentation and personalization profiles:

Note that it is not recommended to change the standard presentation and personalization profiles.

The menu that contains our target logical transactions is QMMAIN. By using drag and drop functionality we can easily add this menu to our profile:

The next step is to ensure that we are accessing the RFUI with those profiles. The presentation profile is assigned to a warehouse in SPRO path SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Mobile Data Entry -> Radio Frequency (RF) Framework -> Define Steps in Logical Transactions -> Assign Presentation Profile to Warehouse:

The personalization profile is assigned to a user in /SCWM/USER transaction:

Upon doing that, we can see that /SCWM/RFUI already shows QM related menus and logical transactions:

Configure and activate Exception Code CRID for the target warehouse in SPRO path SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management-> Cross-Process Settings -> Exception Handling -> Define Exception Codes:

Beyond the configuration described above, all QM configuration is required for the each target Inspection of Type (IOT), which is not covered in this blog post.

For a deep dive into the required configuration for decentral EWM, refer to How to Guide | How To Configure Quality Management Processes for Decentralized EWM Based On SAP S/4HANA. For Embedded EWM, refer to the How to Guide | How To Configure Quality Management Processes for Extended Warehouse Management Embedded in SAP S/4HANA.

3. QM in Embedded EWM vs Decentral EWM - Demo

While Embedded EWM has IOTs 3*, 4 and 5 available for usage, Decentral EWM support the following list:

  • IOT 2: Counting Inbound Delivery
  • IOT 3: Q-Inspection Returns Delivery
  • IOT 4: Q-Inspection Product/Batch Inbound Del.
  • IOT 5: Q-Inspection Product/Batch Whse-Internal
  • IOT 6: Preliminary Inspection Handling Unit

*integrated to the Advanced Return Management (ARM) - See SAP Note 2783648 - Returns inspection process in embedded EWM in SAP S/4HANA.

IOT 1 (Preliminary inspection inbound delivery) is not supported in EWM on S/4HANA (Neither Embedded nor Decentralized).

Understanting this feature difference is important for the next steps of this blog post, as here the main difference between Embedded and Decentral EWM capabilities in regards to mobile QM inspection appear. While in Decentral EWM there is wider range of logical transactions available, including the execution of the inspection process istelf with usage decisions, in Embedded EWM only the creation of the ad hoc inspection document is possible from RFUI, then the inspection execution happens in SAP GUI transactions (e.g. QA02) and Fiori Apps (e.g. Manage Usage Decisions).

The following Quality Management RFUI menus are available for Embedded EWM:

This menu is used to trigger the creation of an ad hoc inspection document, which may immediatelly post the stock to quality stock type depending on the configuration.

The difference between product and HU inspection lies in the object used to search for stock in a given product/batch. By using storage bin inspection, for example, you may inspect a product/batch in a storage bin regardless of the HUs packaging the stock - the entire stock for that material and batch in the entered storage bin is subject for quality inspection process. In the other hand, HU inspection looks only for stock quantities for the product/batch within the informed HU. See next an example for the storage bin option.

Enter Storage bin:

Enter Product/Batch and specify exception code CRID (Create Inspection Document):

See the Inspection document created:

In this example, the stock is automatically moved to quality stock type and can't be used for picking, for example, until a usage decision is made:

In Embedded EWM, there is no way of performing usage decision directly in RFUI. Instead, you need to use either SAP GUI transaction or standard FIORI apps for the mobile execution.

The following Quality Management RFUI menus are available for Decentralized EWM:

As you can see here, beyond "Product Inspection" menu, which is already explained above, we have 3 additional menus available, which only can be seen in a Decentral EWM installation:

HU Inspection with GR Posting

This menu splits in the following options:

This is the menu for Preliminary HU Inspection. The idea here is to search by HUs from inbound process using different objects (ASN, Delivery, HU, TU or Vehicle). When the object is inserted, the system looks for all the deliveries and respective HUs contained on it. Then, it's required to inform all "good" and "bad" HUs as per the following screenshots:

"Bad" HU scanning:

"Good" HU scanning:

F1 button switches from "good" to "bad" HU scanning and vice versa. Attention: It may be required to change screen to adjust "No.Sat.HUs" text as this represent satisfactory HUs in reality (Sat.HUs.).

Upon scanning all HUs, the system posts GR and creates the inspection document only for the satisfactory HUs. The unsatisfactory HUs are not received and therefore are not subject to inspection. This is the benefit of the preliminary inspection - no time is lost receiving, inspecting and eventually reversing GR for HUs that are clearly out of quality standards.

See next the outcome of this process. Received HUs:

Inspection document created:

Product Inspection Decision

Here we have the capability to record decisions for the different IOTs by logging to a work center. For example, we may record the decision for a Quality Inspection document using "Decision by Inspection Document":

Attention: Recording decisions as described above only works if the QIE is not integrated to ERP QM, otherwise the usage decision must be done in SAP ERP side.

QM Counting

This menu is used for QM counting process (IOT 2), which ensures the quality of inbound deliveries by verifying that quantities entered in the system match the physical quantities. It serves as a quality assurance step in the goods receipt process.
Upon changing or activating an inbound delivery notification, the system checks if counting is required by evaluating the inspection object type and rule. If valid, the system creates an inspection document and marks the delivery item for counting. The counting can be done in 2 ways:

  • Explicit counting: Enter count results manually at the counting station, applicable to handling units.
  • Implicit counting: The warehouse task data triggers counting. If differences are detected, the task must be confirmed with an exception code, applicable to unpacked products.

4. Final Considerations

The decision between Embedded vs Decentralized EWM needs to consider all the known differences between the deployment options, such as business fit (that's it, available functionalities in each deployment version), volume and performance, downtime planning, operation risk and long-term IT costs. Quality Management is part of the business fit evaluation criteria. Many other functionallities may be priorital for the decision over QM, but this certainly depends on the business segment and requirements. For example, pharmaceutical industries with high transactional volume would choose Decentral EWM, and may also benefit from the QM advantages of this deployment option.

While Embedded EWM offers simplified QM model by integrating S/4HANA QM engine directly, Decentral EWM offers a wider range of capabilities which come from QIE that exists since old SCM versions of EWM - including the possibility of performing inspection process apart from SAP ERP.

Mobile usage decisions using RFUI may be an advantage over Embedded EWM, but with the evolution of the FIORI apps in QM area, over the time this advantage becomes less expressive. In the other hand, the IOTs available only in Decentralized EWM may be a key factor for the final decision.

5. FAQ and References

How to Guide | How To Configure Quality Management Processes for Extended Warehouse Management Embedded in SAP S/4HANA

How to Guide | How To Configure Quality Management Processes for Decentralized EWM Based On SAP S/4HANA

SAP Help | Quality Management for Embedded EWM

SAP Help | Quality Management for Decentralized EWM

SAP Note 2861813 - "Quality Management (QM) in embedded EWM S/4HANA system"

SAP Note 3376455 - "Release information and restrictions of Decentralized EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS00, FPS01 and FPS02"

entry.title: EWM Warehouse Management Monitor – Enhancements with SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2
entry.content:
The Warehouse Management Monitor in EWM offers a wide range of flexibility for customizing existing monitor node content or creating own monitor content. The how-to guide “How To Add Application Content to the Warehouse Management Monitor” (see note 3004810) guides developers in how to use these options by customizing the standard monitor, adding their own application content to the standard monitor or to completely creating their own monitors / nodes etc.

However, so far the options to do simple enhancements of existing standard nodes, e.g. adding one or two additional fields like customer-defined EEW (Easy Enhancement Workbench) fields, always required* creating customer-specific nodes: It is necessary to create own copies of the existing standard nodes and the underlying source code, and to extend those with the required additional fields. Drawback: You have created Z-coding for the nodes and enhancements or corrections for the EWM standard nodes won’t automatically apply to your copies of the nodes.

*Exception: Nodes that already support “Dynamic Selections” for additional fields, e.g. the Documents nodes for Warehouse Order / Warehouse Task or the Stock and Bin -> Storage Bin node.

With OP2023 FPS2, the new feature “Monitor Screen BAdIs” for the warehouse monitor is introduced, which provides a simplified approach for extending standard nodes without requiring a copy of the standard monitor node anymore. By implementing the new monitor screen BAdI “BAdI: Custom Selection Fields in Standard Monitor Nodes” (/SCWM/EX_MON_CUSTOM_SELECT), it is possible extend the selection screens of selected standard monitor nodes with customer-defined fields, and to either integrate them into the standard selection logic, or to implement a customer-specific selection logic for the new fields that can be combined with the standard selection logic. This new feature is described in the latest version of the Monitor How-To guide, version 3.0.

Example

Example Use Case: You have extended Inbound Deliveries with a customer-specific EEW-Field “Priority” and Handling Units with a customer-specific EEW-Field “Preferred Putaway Destination”. You want to use both fields in the warehouse management monitor to be able to select e.g. Inbound Deliveries of priority 1 and 2, and to select Handling Units relevant for such deliveries that are intended to go to specific putaway destinations in your warehouse.

Prerequisite: You have already created domains and data elements for the EEW fields and extended the structures with the respective fields. You have also defined logical field names for the delivery specific EEW fields. You have implemented the necessary application logic to write and read the new fields during Inbound Delivery and Handling Unit processing.

You can use the new Monitor Screen BAdIs to enhance the Inbound Delivery selection screen with the new selection field for “Priority”, and the selection screen for the Handling Unit subnode with both the additional parent selection field “Priority” and the HU specific selection field for the “Preferred putaway destination”. The extended screens could look like this:

Figure 1 - Standard Inbound Delivery selection screen with customer specific Screen Enhancement

Figure 2 - Standard Inbound Delivery -> HU selection screen with customer specific Screen Enhancement

Steps to extend a Monitor Node with a Screen BAdI

In the latest version of the Monitor How-To Guide, you can find a detailed example of how to enhance an existing standard monitor node with customer specific fields. In addition, you can always check the example implementations for the new BAdI for guidance on how to implement each BAdI method per node. For the example use case described above, you can e.g. check  /SCWM/CL_EI_MON_CUST_SEL_INB.

Roughly summarized, the following needs to be done to enhance the standard nodes for the example Use Case described above:

  1. Creation of a BAdI implementation and implementing class, specific for your warehouse number and the technical names of the to-be-extended nodes as filters: /SCWM/WHRITEM_MON + /SCWM/WHRHEAD_INB_HU_MON.
    • Recommendation: Implement one BAdI for hierarchical monitor nodes, e.g. Inbound or Outbound Delivery Order and their respective sub nodes. This ensures that the defined selection screens and selection fields can be reused and are handled consistently for all nodes.
  2. Creation of a Z-report to define the customer specific selection screen for your EEW-fields, and to implement the screen and variant data handling. The selection screen will be included in the standard monitor node in a new subscreen area, which is located below the standard selection screens. Check the details in the Monitor How-To Guide and the comments in the example implementations for detailed information how to implement the Z-report including the sub screen.

Please note:

  • For simplicity, we will only describe the variant with EEW fields in this document, but you can use the following fields for extending the selection screens:
    1.      Standard fields for the objects that are not included in the standard selection screen
    2.      EEW-fields added to the EEW include of the relevant objects
  • If you use such fields, they can be considered in the standard selection of the monitor node. Other fields won’t be considered, as there are validity checks in place that will filter out fields that can’t be supported by the standard selection logic.
  • For the example described in this document, you can therefore use fields from /SCWM/S_WIP_Q_WHR_INBOUND (incl. EEW fields) for the Inbound Delivery selection screen, and /SCWM/S_HUHDR_MON (incl. EEW fields) for the HU selection screen.
  1. Implementation of the BAdI methods according to your use case.
    Please note: For most nodes, it is sufficient to implement the BAdI and the customer specific report to provide the mapping for the additional EEW-fields to the standard node. The standard selection logic can directly consider the additional selection values. This does not apply for HU-specific selections, though – for HU-specific nodes, an additional customer-specific pre-selection must be implemented in the BAdI. You can find details in the Monitor How-To Guide and also in the node specific example implementations.  
    • The pre-selection logic should return HU keys that are relevant based on the customer selection values. It is recommended to carefully design this pre-selection logic, as it might have an impact on the performance of the overall node execution. Please note that if the pre-selection does not return any results, the standard selection will not continue, either.
    • The maximum number of returned HU keys is 32.000, if this is exceeded the monitor selection will not continue
    • The pre-selection logic will only be called if customer specific selection fields are used. If only standard fields are used, the standard selection will be executed and the pre-selection logic will be skipped.
  2. Specifying the screen enhancements in the BADI implementation, to connect standard screen and custom subscreen area from the Z-report.

You can find the screens that should be enhanced with to custom subscreen area in the BAdI Implementation section “Screen Enhancement”

Figure 3 - Example Screen Enhancements for Inbound Delivery node examples

Conclusion

With the new Monitor Screen BAdI feature, simple selection screen enhancements of existing standard monitor nodes are now possible without the need to copy existing nodes to customer nodes. This reduces the overhead when implementing corrections or upgrading, as the standard nodes will get any corrections and enhancements, and no customer nodes need to be adapted in addition.

Monitor nodes extended by customer selection fields still behave like any other standard monitor node, e.g. you can use standard functionalities like setting up automatic refresh, creating selection variants for the node incl. the customer selection fields, scheduling background executions of the variant selections, etc., if the Screen BAdI is correctly implemented.

In which cases does it make sense to use the Screen BAdI?

  • Simple enhancements of existing nodes, where standard selections can include the fields, e.g. Inbound Delivery Items with EEW fields
  • Simple enhancements of existing nodes, where HU pre-selection can be implemented in a simple way to pre-select values based on customer fields (e.g. selecting HUs with additional standard selection fields, e.g. open WTs, or HU EEW fields).

In which cases does it not make sense?

  • Enhancements of nodes with fields that are not supported by the original node selection, e.g. because the requirement is to combine selection information of multiple objects, or the selection fields is not even a standard field (e.g. enhancing the HU document bin selection with a field for number of completed warehouse tasks)
  • Enhancements of nodes with a complex set of selection criteria, or special requirements for the selection that does not match the logic of the standard monitor node.

In theses cases, the creation of an own monitor node with a customized selection logic is recommended. The Monitor How-To Guide provides detailed examples for the creation of customer specific nodes as well.

FIND OUT MORE!

To see more details on the above mentioned topics (such as examples of configuration screens), as well as see what other enhancements we delivered with this release, I highly encourage you to explore our blog SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Extended Warehouse Management Private Edition 2023 FPS02– What’s New .

You can also find more technical details and documentation on the above listed topic and others we left out via the “What’s New” viewer on the Help Documentation portal.

For more information about SAP Extended Warehouse Management, please follow us on social media, our YouTube channel or our community pages:

SAP EWM Community

SAP YouTube Channel

EWM LinkedIn Community

SAP Extended Warehouse Management Roadmap Explorer

Release information and restrictions of embedded EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023

Release information and restrictions of Decentralized EWM on SAP S/4HANA 2023

For a complete list of Q&A from the EWM community, please access this link:
https://answers.sap.com/tags/01200615320800000705

In case you do not find your specific question there, feel free to post your question via the following form:
https://answers.sap.com/questions/ask.html?primaryTagId=01200615320800000705

entry.title: Complex Inbound Process with SAP EWM: POSC, LOSC & Quality Integration
entry.content:
Let's assume a scenario in an automotive spare parts warehouse, where efficiency and precision are paramount, especially when handling a variety of components ranging from hefty engine blocks to small yet essential items like spark plugs and brake pads

Step 1: Arrival and Unloading

The process begins when a consolidated shipment arrives from the vendor. After unloading, goods are temporarily placed in the Goods Receipt Zone, ready for further processing.

Step 2: Deconsolidation – Sorting by Size and Type
  • Insp. Planning W/o Rules

At the Deconsolidation Work Center, the shipment is broken down into handling units (HUs) based on size and type, ensuring storage efficiency and traceability.

Step 3: Quality Inspection

Each HU undergoes a thorough Quality Inspection to ensure standards are met before storage. Only approved items proceed.

Step 4: Putaway

Warehouse tasks guide operators to place:

  • Large Components in the Bulk Storage Area.
  • Small Components in High Rack Storage or Mezzanine levels, depending on the requirement.

By combining POSC and LOSC with Quality Management Integration, SAP EWM ensures accurate, error-free inbound processes, improving overall warehouse efficiency and maintaining strict quality standards.

Configuration

1. Process-Oriented Storage Control: 

Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC) involves assigning a sequence of specific steps to be carried out in the warehouse after products are received.

  • Path: SPRO--> IMG-->SCM EWM-->EWM-->Cross Processing Setting-->Warehouse Task--> Define Process-Oriented Storage Control.
  • External Storage process step

  • Storage process- Definition

 

  •  
  • Assign Storage Process Step

  • Process Oriented Storage control

 

 

 

 

 

 

2. Warehouse Process Type

A Warehouse Process Type is a classification that defines the type of movement and handling required within a warehouse. It specifies the nature of material flows, such as putaway, stock transfer, or picking, and is used to guide the system in executing these movements efficiently.

Path: SPRO-->IMG-->SCM EWM-->EWM-->Cross Processing Settings--> Warehouse Task-->Warehouse Process Type

  • Define Warehouse process type

Acceptance WPT

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Rejection WPT

  • Control Indicator for warehouse process type

  • Determine Warehouse Process Type

 

 

 

 

3. Work Center

A Work Center in the warehouse is a designated area for specific physical activities, replicated in EWM to mirror the actual workflow. It enables the system to recognize the activities performed there, allowing warehouse associates to update information directly into the system.

  • Two activities must be completed in the warehouse before putaway: deconsolidation and quality inspection. This requires setting up two separate work centers one for deconsolidation and another for quality inspection
 I. Quality Inspection Work Center
  • Define Storage type for QI Work center, Create a Bin and do bin sortin
  • Define Work Center

        Path: SPRO--> IMG-->SCM EWM--> EWM--> Master Data--> Work Center--> Define Work Center

  • Assign bin to work center- /N/SCWM/TWORKST

 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II. Deconsolidation Work Center
  • Define Work Center

  • Assign Work Center to the Work Center Bin- /N/SCWM/TWORKST

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

III. ID point Work Center

  • Define Work Center

  • Assign Work Center to the Work Center Bin- /N/SCWM/TWORKST

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

4. Put Away Strategies

Put away strategies are essential in EWM, ensuring that products are efficiently directed to their designated storage areas. The storage types have been defined as follows: HA10, HA20, and HA30 for high-rack storage, HA40 for bulk storage, and RJCT for rejected products.

Path: SPRO-->IMG-->SCM EWM-->EWM-->Goods receipt processing--> Strategies--> Storage type Search

  • Define storage type search sequence for put away

Acceptance STSS

Rejection STSS

  • Assign Storage type for storage type search sequence

Acceptance

Rejection

  • Put away Control Indicator

Control Indicators are considered same for acceptance and rejection.

  • Specify Storage type search sequence

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

5. Deconsolidation Attributes

Path: SPRO-->IMG-->SCM EWM-->EWM--> Goods Receipt Processes-->Deconsolidation

  • Define Attributes for Deconsolidation

  • Specify Deconsolidation station

  • Pull Deconsolidation Number to Activity Area

6. Quality Management set up

Quality Management (QM) integration is essential for effective quality inspection processes within the warehouse.
