Configurations on both SAP EWM and SAP S/4HANA sides must be set up to ensure a seamless flow of quality data and processes between systems. This integration enables real-time quality control within the warehouse, allowing for efficient handling of inspection results and facilitating smooth movement of goods based on quality decisions.

I. EWM Quality Management
  • Generate Inspection object types version

Path: SPRO-->IMG--> SCM EWM--> EWM--> Cross Processing settings--> Quality Management--> Basics and Integration--> Generate Inspection object type version

  • Maintain Inspection Object type- IOT-04

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> SCM EWM--> EWM--> Cross processing Settings--> Quality Management--> Basics and Integration--> Maintain Inspection Object types versions

  • Define and Activate warehouse dependent IOTs

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> SCM EWM --> EWM--> Cross processing Settings--> Quality Management--> Basics and Integration--> Define and Activate warehouse dependent IOTs

Warehouse Dependent IOT Settings

Insp. Planning W/o Rules

  • Maintain Inspection Rule Group

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> SCM EWM --> EWM--> Cross processing Settings--> Quality Management--> Inspection Planning--> Maintain Inspection Rule Group

  • Maintain Follow-up Action

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> SCM EWM --> EWM--> Cross processing Settings--> Quality Management--> Inspection Result--> Maintain Follow-up Action--> Define Follow-up Actions

Define Follow-Up Actions for Inspection

  • Assign Logistical Follow-up Actions

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> SCM EWM --> EWM--> Cross processing Settings--> Quality Management--> Inspection Result--> Maintain Follow-up Action--> Assign Logistical Follow-up Actions

 

 II. S4 Quality Management

  • Maintain Settings at Plant Level

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> Quality Management--> Basic Settings--> Maintain settings at plant level

  • Define Follow Up Actions for Usage Decision

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> Quality Management--> Inspection lot completion--> Define Follow Up Actions for Usage Decisions

  • Edit Code Groups and code for usage decisions

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> Quality Management-->Inspection completion--> Edit Code Groups and code for usage decisions

  • Code Groups for usage decision

  • Codes for Usage Decision

  • Edit Selected sets for usage Decisions

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> Quality Management-->Inspection completion-->Edit Selected sets for usage Decisions

  • Selected sets for Usage Decision

  • Selected set codes for Usage Decision

7. Maintain Material & Product Master

Proper configuration of Material and Product Masters is essential, especially for quality inspection processes. Maintaining quality information in the Material Master is crucial, and all control indicators should also be accurately configured in the Product Master to ensure seamless operations.

  • Material Master – MM01

Quality Management view

The 1701 inspection type is assigned to the material master under the Quality Management view. Similarly, all materials subject to inspection have been assigned the same inspection type. Due to the length of the blog, not all screenshots have been included.

  • Product Master- /N/SCWM/MAT1

Each product subject to quality inspection in the product master should be assigned its respective process type indicators and put away controls. Additionally, assigning the quality inspection group is essential. To maintain brevity, other screenshots have been omitted and listed in table below.

Product

PTDI

PACI

Quality Insp. Grp

Bulk Indicator

000000000000001351- Engine Blocks

ME

EB

QUAL

BL

000000000000001352- Spark Plugs

ME

F1

QUAL

-

000000000000001353- Oil Filters

ME

OF

QUAL

-

000000000000001354- Brake Pads

ME

BP

QUAL

-

8. Maintain Inspection Rule in EWM

T.code: /N/SCWM/QRSETUP

In this step, we define that a particular product is associated with the INB document type and IDLV item type, along with the Quality Inspection group QUAL and 1701 inspection type. This setup indicates that if the product arrives with this specified information, it will be subject to a quality inspection in EWM.

9. Layout- Oriented Storage Control (LOSC)

Create storage type and bins for a mezzanine floor (HAF1), generate an activity area (HAF1), and perform bin sorting.

  • Define Layout-Oriented Storage process Control

Path: SPRO--> IMG--> SCM EWM--> EWM--> Cross processing settings--> warehouse Task-->Define Layout Oriented Storage Process Control

Testing

 

  1. Create Purchase Order -ME21N

The following four products will be sent together to the warehouse

Product

Quantity

000000000000001351- Engine Blocks

1

000000000000001352- Spark Plugs

1

000000000000001353- Oil Filters

1

s000000000000001354- Brake Pads

1

 

  1. Create Inbound delivery & distribute to EWM- VL31N

Product

Quantity

HU#

000000000000001351- Engine Blocks

1

700817

000000000000001352- Spark Plugs

1

 

700818

000000000000001353- Oil Filters

1

000000000000001354- Brake Pads

1

Engine blocks are packed in a separate handling unit (HU) due to their weight, while the remaining products are grouped in another HU. These two HUs, containing all four products, will be sent to the warehouse.

  1. Maintain Inbound delivery in EWM- /N/SCWM/PRDI

Two HUs with four products are distributed to EWM

  1. Create Unload Warehouse Task

Two warehouse tasks are created separately for each HU, using process type ZMEC and storage process ZMEC.

For wider visibility, let’s open this Inbound delivery in SAP Monitor

  • /N/SCWM/MON

A warehouse task is created with the storage process ZMEC, starting with the initial step to unload using the external step IBUL, moving items from the door to the staging area. Upon confirming these tasks, four pending tasks and two open tasks are generated, as shown in the below image.

Open tasks involve moving HUs from the staging area to the Deconsolidation work center, designated as storage type 8010. Pending tasks are those awaiting put away to the final storage bin. Since there are four products in the inbound delivery, four pending tasks have been created.

 

Let's confirm the tasks for moving the HUs to the Deconsolidation work center. All open tasks are now confirmed, but the pending tasks still remain. The warehouse associate must deconsolidate the items and update the system, which can be done using transaction code /N/SCWM/DCONS.

5. Deconsolidation- /N/SCWM/DCONS.

The Deconsolidation work center is DMEC. By entering the warehouse number and work center details, the warehouse associate can update the information.

As the inbound delivery consists of two HUs, those are listed here as shown in the image below.

 

Now let’s put each product in each HU and process it by clicking on “Complete process step for HU”. More HUs can be created using “Create HU” section on right side of the screen as shown in image.

Upon clicking "Complete Process Step," follow-up tasks will be generated automatically. Let's return to the monitor to check.

As observed, the pending tasks still remain. Additionally, four more tasks have been created for Quality Inspection with the external step "QIS." These open tasks are for moving products from the Deconsolidation work center (8010) to the Quality Work Center (8020).

Let’s confirm those four tasks to move to quality work center an open Quality Work Center.

6. Quality Inspection Work Center- /N/SCWM/QINSP_S4

Work Center is “QINC” and storage type is 8020

As shown in the image below, four products are ready for inspection. The associate will conduct the inspection, make a decision, and update the information in the system.

In the Quality Inspection Workload Overview screen, the warehouse associate can perform a mass inspection for multiple items by selecting “Mass Inspection for Selected Items.” This action opens the decision panel, where the associate can make quality-related decisions for each item. From the decision panel, choices are made using specific codes within the code group:

  • A1 represents "Accepted – unrestricted stock" for items that pass inspection.
  • R1 represents "Rejected – blocked stock" for items that fail inspection.

By selecting the appropriate code, the associate updates the inspection status in the system, ensuring that each item is routed accordingly based on the inspection outcome. This streamlined process allows for efficient handling of inspection decisions for multiple items at once.

In this quality inspection process, the associate evaluates each product and selects an appropriate decision based on the inspection results.

  • For the first three products (Automotive Engine Block, Spark Plug, and Oil Filters). These items passed inspection. The associate selects the decision code "A1" (Acceptance) in the "Decision" field, which automatically triggers the follow-up action "EWM_AP". This action ensures that accepted products proceed to the next step in the warehouse workflow.

For the fourth product (Automotive Brake Pads): This item failed the inspection. The associate selects the decision code "R1" (Rejection) in the "Decision" field, which triggers the follow-up action "EWM_RP". This action routes the rejected product to a designated rejected storage area for further handling or rework.

Clicking on “Close HU” completes the process and saves the information.

Returning to the Monitor, observe that one pending task has been canceled due to rejection. This task is redirected to the RJCT storage type, and a new task has been created with the activity “STCH” for posting change, directing it to blocked stock. All remaining pending tasks are open for put away to their respective storage bins EXCEPT one Warehouse task is still pending because, it should be going to Mezzanine floor, LOSC takes from here for that storage bin, system generated additional tasks to move from ID point HAID.  

All tasks have been confirmed, and items have been moved to their final destination bins EXCEPT Spark plug , that is in ID point as shown in below image. All stock types are marked as F1, except for one item labeled B5, which was rejected during inspection. This blocked stock (B5) is excluded from picking operations.

Let's check the status of the inbound delivery in transaction /N/SCWM/PRDI. The Goods Receipt, Unloading, Put away, and Warehouse activities have been completed. Now, let's return to the S4 system to verify if it has been updated accordingly.

The information has been successfully updated in SAP S/4HANA, triggering movement types 411 and 325. Movement type 411 is used for transferring special stock, such as consignment or project stock, to the company's own unrestricted-use stock. Movement type 325 facilitates the transfer of materials from one storage location to another within the same plant, specifically moving items into blocked stock

The Material Document List (MB51) displays the movement types used for various automotive parts at the HAAP Plant, tracking each item's stock status based on inspection results. Each material, such as the Automotive Engine Block, Spark Plug, Oil Filters, and Brake Pads, initially received a 101-movement type upon goods receipt into the warehouse. Following inspection, accepted items (Engine Block, Spark Plug, and Oil Filters) were assigned a 411-movement type to indicate a posting change to unrestricted-use stock, making them available for use or distribution. The Automotive Brake Pads, however, failed inspection and were first assigned a 344-movement type to move them to blocked stock, followed by a 325 movement type for further classification in restricted stock. This process ensures that rejected items are excluded from regular operations, allowing for effective stock management and quality control within the warehouse.

entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 23 - Get your goods ready for a trip with the SAP Route!
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 23 – Get your goods ready for a trip with the SAP Route!

You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

The idea of a route! 

A route basically describes a defined path of a journey or of a trip. When companies ship goods, they also send them on a journey. To make this journey as efficient, cost saving, safe and fast as possible it is necessary to find the right route.

This blog post is about the route in SAP and which processes are affected by the route. We also discuss the differences between routes in SAP ERP and SAP EWM and about the integration of SAP Transportation Management. 

The Route in SAP ERP

Let’s first clarify the idea of a route or route determination in SAP. The route is about clarifying what is to be transported - how - where from and where to.

Let's make an example: We get a customer order and the customer wants to have the goods tomorrow. This means that the goods must be picked and shipped promptly. It is necessary to create an express order in our SAP system with a corresponding shipping condition. Let's pick up on the four questions again:

What - this are the ordered goods that have transport requirements due to their characteristics - e.g. bulky goods can only be transported by freight forwarder.
How - this describes the shipping condition, e.g. “by twelve o'clock tomorrow” or in other words - express delivery.
Where from - our warehouse.
Where to - this is the customer's shipping address.

Define Route in SAP S/4HANA

 Based on these information, we can now start planning the journey for our goods that means we can determine a route in SAP based on the master data and defined rules. 

Route determination in Sales Orders

The transportation costs are then also calculated from the route - we all know this when we privately select “Express shipping” in the webshop, for example. In this case, the transportation costs can be determined directly from the route. The route also determines the means of transport and the involved stages. It also influences the order scheduling that means when can we deliver with regard to how long does it take to transport the goods to their destination. This transportation lead time can be used to carry out backward scheduling to determine when goods need to be loaded in order to meet the desired delivery date.

Transportation and Delivery Scheduling in S/4HANA based on route information

Different sales order items can also have different routes if, for example, bulky goods and small parts are in one order but it could be that we send them out differently for example the small parts with a parcel service provider and the bulky goods with a freight forwarder. 

It is important to say that the route is not real transportation planning, as the route is only determined at item level and does not carry out any cross-item or cross-order planning. That only happens during transportation planning.

Let's have a closer look to important terms which were already mentioned: 

Shipping condition -  describes the “General shipping strategy for goods to deliver them from the supplier to the customer for example ‘Standard’ or ‘Express shipping’ strategy. The shipping condition can  be maintained directly in the customer master data and is then used for sales order creation or it can be linked to the order type.

Shipping type - specifies the means of transport used for transportation for example ship by truck, rail, ship or plane.

Transportation group - summarizes the properties that are important for shipping the goods. For example, it can indicate properties such as “liquid” or “to be transported cooled”. The transportation group is also maintained in the material master since it describes material characteristics.

Means of transport - describes a grouping of resources for example cool-trucks, 40-ton trucks or 20-feet containers.

Means of transport type - groups together different means of transport that are of the same type for example all available trucks are assigned to the means of transport type “Truck” which can be a 40-ton truck or a cooled truck.

Route determination for sales order items uses order header data and material master data. The shipping country and the destination country or the corresponding regions as well as the shipping condition come from the order header. The transportation group is taken from the material master of the corresponding item. As mentioned before customers could have different priorities for different sales order items and therefore different shipping conditions can be relevant and it is also possible to determine different routes for different order items.

During outbound delivery creation a new route determination takes place. And this determination also considers the delivery weight in addition. Because the delivery contains all the goods which are shipped together and therefore the relevant shipping weight is now available and can influence of course also the route determination.

Route determination in SAP S/4HANA for outbound deliveries

The route in the outbound delivery is now a header information. That means that there is at least a separate outbound delivery for each route. The delivery then uses the characteristics of the route like transit time or transportation lead time to plan the shipping.

There are also many companies who work with regional freight forwarders. The route in SAP ERM is often  used in that case to group deliveries according to regions. Companies then define, for example, the northern route, the west route, the south route and so on. If, for example, a shipper uses a specific forwarder for domestic transport from its distribution center to all cities in the western region, the shipper can define receiving zones for route determination for example all cities in US that begin with zip code 9. The receiving zones can then be used to find specific routes and the corresponding freight forwarders via the route.

Everything we have discussed so far is known as “SD route” or “LE-TRA route”. Both terms are used, but they mean the same SAP object!

Route and route determination in SAP S/4HANA

 The SCM-Route in SAP EWM

First of all: Route determination in EWM is independent of route determination in ERP! That means there are different determination frameworks, different route objects and there is also no distribution of the ERP master data SD route to EWM! The Supply Chain Management route (SCM-Route) in EWM is a completely independent object! And it can of course make sense to carry out another route determination in the warehouse management system, that means in EWM. Just think about the weight or quantity which may have changed because certain products cannot be delivered as desired.  

But EWM can also work with the SD route. The SD route is send to EWM within the outbound delivery and additional information such as the times from scheduling as “planned goods issue “ is also available in the EWM outbound delivery. Customers can decide via configuration in EWM which route they want to use.

SCM Route in SAP EWM

 How is the SCM-Route determined in EWM?

The SCM route is determined with the so called “Routing Guide”! The determination logic is very similar to ERP. The routing guide uses:

  • Ship-from location and Ship-to location
  • Shipping condition
  • Transportation group

This is more or less the same as for the SD route determination.

In addition, the determination in EWM can check whether the SCM-Route is suitable for dangerous goods. Therefore the dangerous goods master data in EH&S is used. There are also other criteria that can be taken into account for the determination:

  • Minimum or maximum weight or volume per item or per delivery
  • Maximum length/width/height of the products

The SCM-Route determination is therefore more granular than the SD-Route determination and therefore it allows a more specific planning. In addition, transportation costs can be taken into account in the determination that means the most cost-effective route can be determined.

Route Determination in SAP EWM with SCM Routing Guide

The determination takes place automatically when the EWM outbound delivery order is created and it can also be manually triggered later. In EWM there is only one route field in the EWM delivery order header, so there can only be one route and customer can decide via configuration if they want to use the SD-Route or the SCM-Route.

What is the route actually used for in EWM – independent if it’s the SD or SCM route?

The route can be used for example for wave determination. All deliveries of certain routes can be grouped into the same waves. And the route also influences scheduling, because the route is used to determine the time at which the delivery should leave the warehouse for example via the departure calendar of the SCM Route. 

The route can also be used in EWM to plan EWM transportation units. For this purpose, there is a report in EWM where the route can be used as a main selection criteria.

The route can also be used in consolidation group determination, e.g. to determine which deliveries can be packed together. And the route can also be taken into account in packaging planning and the route can then be used to determine special work centers as part of work center determination. The creation of pick orders can also be influenced by the route. 

Another important point is staging zone determination, which can be influenced by the route. This allows deliveries on certain routes to be directed to specially designated zones. The creation of an invoice before goods issue can also be activated for certain routes. And in loading, the route can be used to check whether unplanned loading is permitted on an existing shipment in EWM.

Example:  Let's assume we have received a customer order and we have determined a route that specifies shipping via a certain parcel service provider and tomorrow is the desired delivery date. We have swap bodies at dedicated warehouse doors for this parcel service provider which are picked up by the service provider at fixed times.

Based on this information we can find an appropriate wave via the route and the planned goods issue time of the delivery item ensures that we find a wave which is picked, packed, staged and loaded according to the desired delivery date. After picking, the packages are moved to workstations that are specially equipped for parcel shipping. The appropriate staging zone and the door where the swap bodies are waiting are determined based on the route. All determinations happen completely automatically in the background and ensure a smooth delivery in time. 

It is easy to see that the route is an object that has major influence on the shipping processes in SAP EWM. The route in EWM takes over dispatch control if no explicit transportation planning takes place.

SAP EWM uses the SCM route since the beginning. However, in 2016 it was not initially adopted into EWM on S/4HANA. Since the release S/4HANA 2021, the SCM Route is now also available in EWM on S/4HANA - both embedded and decentralized.

The SCM route is also used as part of the Global ATP (Available-to-Promise), which takes place in SAP APO. It provides information on transportation and therefore enables a more precise delivery date commitment. And that is why we talk about the SCM route and not the EWM route, because it is historically an SCM object and is still used by other SCM modules too.

Transportation Cross-Docking is based on the SCM-Route 

Transportation cross-docking is one of the planned cross-docking scenarios in SAP EWM. With transportation cross docking, the ordered goods do not go directly from the initial warehouse to the end customer, but they are routed via one or more intermediate locations without having to be repacked there. Reason for the process may be, for example, that several deliveries are consolidated, the goods are processed centrally for export or the means of transportation is simply changed.

In case of a customer order, the initial EWM managed warehouse which ships the goods receives the outbound delivery to the customer. EWM then carries out a route determination and EWM finds a SCM-Route which is transportation cross-docking relevant and together with the route an intermediate warehouse location is determined - the so-called cross-docking warehouse.

The location of the interim warehouse becomes the actual ship-to-party of the outbound delivery in EWM and the original final customer becomes the final-ship-to-party of the delivery. Follow-up documents for cross-docking are then automatically created in SAP ERP with the goods issue posting of the initial outbound delivery. The follow up documents are an inbound delivery for the cross-docking warehouse and an outbound delivery from the cross-docking warehouse to the final goods recipient. Both deliveries know each other and they are distributed as a delivery couple to the EWM-managed cross-docking warehouse. These special deliveries are marked as Cross Docking delivers and they can trigger special cross-docking or consolidation processes in EWM.

There is also a separate transportation cross-docking monitor in SAP ERP that you can use to monitor the progress of the cross-docking process. Transportation cross-docking is not only for sales orders but can also be used for stock transport orders too. The trigger is the SCM route determination in the initial warehouse which has to be managed by SAP EWM.

How do the route and transport planning fit together?

At SAP there are two solutions that we can use to plan and manage transportation. SAP Transportation Management and LE-TRA. LE-TRA stands for Logistics Execution Transportation.

SAP Transportation Management is SAP's strategic solution for this topic. LE-TRA as compatibility scope in S/4HANA can only be used in SAP S/4HANA until the end of 2030. The SD-Route or LE-TRA-Route in ERP is not affected by the end of the usage rights and can be used in order documents and delivery documents even after 2030! (see SAP Note 2270199)

Shipment planning with LE-TRA

If LE-TRA is used, the route can be used for example as a selection criteria during shipment creation. Transportation planners can select all non-assigned outbound deliveries with a specific route to combine them into one shipment. Using the LE-TRA shipment as a bracket for outbound deliveries in order to process them together, print delivery documents etc. is certainly one of the most common use cases of SAP customers.

The route can then be transferred from the deliveries to the shipment header as shipment route (LE-TRA-Route). As mentioned at the beginning, the route also defines the means of transport for example by truck, rail, ship or plane. - and the route can be also be used for transportation scheduling that means how long will the shipment take including administrative effort and so on. And the route can also be used to calculate transportation costs for the shipment.

Transportation Management with SAP TM

In SAP TM the route object no longer exists. In TM there is the so-called “Standard or default route”, but this has nothing to do with the SD-Route or SCM-Route. It’s only a simplification of transportation planning in TM: In short, the simplification is - that the optimizer does not always have to be triggered to perform simple planning. Among other things, the default route is used for sales order scheduling for example.

Sales order scheduling supports the delivery date determination in the ERP sales order. It is comparable to the determination via the SD-Route. However sales order scheduling uses information from SAP Transportation Management which offers for example a better forecast of the required transportation times or it is necessary for customers who only use SAP TM and no SD-Routes for example. 

Sales order scheduling with SAP TM is based on strong communication between SAP ERP and SAP TM. First, backward scheduling is performed in SAP TM starting from the requested delivery date in order to determine the pick date or availability date of the goods. If the availability check (the ATP in ERP) cannot confirm this date, SAP TM now determines a new possible delivery date via forward scheduling - based on the committed material availability.

This feature shows perfectly the strength of SAP: the deep and native integration of different SAP modules. In this case SAP Sales Distribution, the available to promise and SAP Transportation management in order to determine the most reliable delivery promise for customers.

As mentioned above SAP TM follows a different approach compared to LE-TRA and it works with locations, transportation zones, stages and so on. And the various transportation demands or freight units are then turned into one or more freight orders with start and destination locations in TM, which are approached in a specific sequence. This planning can be done manually or with the help of the TM optimizer. There are also schedules and much much more in SAP TM. As you can see from the explanation, the route no longer plays a role in TM. 

The planning results are for example freight orders with start and destination locations. These freight orders are the objects which are used for the TM-EWM integration. This means that SAP EWM does not receive any further routes from the TM system but rather a freight order with assigned delivery items, time information, carrier information, loading sequence and so on. 

What are the consequences for warehouse planning and operations?

If a freight order exists in SAP TM and this freight order is send to the warehouse, EWM can now control many processes based on the freight order instead of using the route. Processes like wave determination, warehouse operations scheduling or staging zone and door determination can be done with regard to the transport instead of the route.

And this is independent of the used integration scenario between EWM and SAP TM – that means it works with the classic EWM-TM integration using Transportation Units and also with Advanced Shipping and Receiving where EWM works directly on the TM freight order.

Conclusion

The route in SAP Logistics is an important tool to control shipping processes in SAP ERP as well as in SAP EWM. With the native integration of SAP Transportation Management and SAP Extended Warehouse Management there are new possibilities to manage the shipping of goods based on freight orders. 

Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: Customer Onboarding for SAP S/4HANA PCE for Extended Warehouse Management Extra-Stack
entry.content:
Kickstart Your SAP EWM Journey with Confidence!

Are you a new SAP EWM customer gearing up for your project implementation? Starting off on the right foot is crucial to ensuring a smooth and successful SAP experience. That’s why we’ve created the Customer Onboarding Journey, tailored specifically for the SAP Extended Warehouse Management (SAP EWM) solution.

What’s Your Next Step?

  1. Explore the SAP EWM Onboarding Resource Center

Your ultimate one-stop shop for a hassle-free onboarding process. Here, you’ll find ungated enablement resources designed to set you up for success, including:

  • Step-by-step guides on using SAP for Me
  • Instructions on how to access your system
  • Detailed insights into what to expect and how to prepare for your project implementation
  1. Join the Onboarding Webcasts

These quarterly live sessions are crafted to help you navigate the onboarding process with ease. You’ll gain valuable insights, have your questions answered, and get up to speed on all things SAP EWM.

  • Can’t make it live? Don’t worry—on-demand recordings are available whenever you need them.

Your Onboarding Journey: Four Phases to Success

The content in the SAP EWM Onboarding Resource Center and Onboarding Webcasts is designed to align with your journey, guiding you through every phase of your onboarding experience.

Ready to make the most of your SAP EWM project? Start exploring today and set the stage for future success:

This is the content in more detail:

Phase 1. Get Started with SAP 

Objective: Help you understand the SAP EWM provisioning process, familiarize you with key SAP channels, and enable smooth access to systems and support.

Key Highlights:

  • Grasp your onboarding journey essentials
  • Navigate SAP channels like SAP for Me and set up your s-user
  • Gain system access without hassle
  • Learn how to use SAP Support for all your technical and non-technical needs

 

Phase 2. Plan your Implementation

Objective: Equip you with the knowledge to effectively plan, manage, and accelerate your SAP EWM project using the tools included in your subscription.

 Key Highlights:

  • Implement your project with the SAP Activate methodology
  • Manage tasks efficiently using SAP Cloud ALM
  • Leverage SAP Best Practices to streamline your project
  • Discover tools for seamless data migration
  • Understand the importance of roles and system testing

 

Phase 3. Get Enabled

Objective: Empower you with free learning resources and tools to master your SAP EWM solution.

Key Highlights:

  • Dive into the SAP EWM Solution Overview
  • Explore comprehensive guides in the SAP Help Portal
  • Connect with peers and experts in the SAP Community
  • Take advantage of learning opportunities through SAP Enterprise Support

 

Phase 4. Prepare to Go Live

Objective: Ensure a seamless Go-Live experience while setting you up for long-term success.

Key Highlights:

  • Tackle final project tasks with confidence
  • Access SAP services and offerings for Go-Live support
  • Utilize post-live resources for continuous improvement
  • Stay ahead by embracing innovation after Go-Live

In Conclusion

The SAP EWM onboarding journey is thoughtfully crafted to support you from the moment your contract is signed to your successful Go-Live. Take advantage of every phase to explore, plan, and engage with the resources available.

Your journey to SAP EWM success begins now—let’s make it happen!

#FirstExperiencesLast

#SAP

#EWM

entry.title: EWM Form & Label Print through PPF
entry.content:
Introduction

EWM uses the Post Processing Framework (PPF) to specify whether PPF is able to perform a PPF action (for example, printing), within the PPF, EWM calls determination of the condition technique in order to find the appropriate condition records for performing a PPF action.

In this post, I would like to introduce how to set up Forms & Labels print in EWM. This blog will not go in detail how to create Form or create custom PPF but about assign Form and setup condition record to trigger Form/Label printing. Example is based on Standard form.

This post includes below Form/Label:

1. Warehouse Order/ Warehouse Task print

2. Handling Unit print

3. TU Loading list print

4. Physical Inventory count sheet print

5. Delivery Note print

Prerequisites

Defined warehouse-number-specific print settings (in particular the spool parameters).

Tcode: /SCWM/60000431 - Maintain Warehouse-Specific Printing Parameters

Create spool and assign default printer

Print Warehouse Order/ Warehouse Task

EWM can use this function to automatically print lists during creation of warehouse orders and warehouse tasks. These lists contain information about the storage bin to and from which a warehouse employee should move specific goods.

From warehouse management monitor, can manually print a warehouse order, which also prints all corresponding warehouse tasks. For example, the first printout is damaged, or your require a second printout.

Example scenarios to trigger WO Print:

  • Putaway label - WO Single - Contents HU number, Product, quantity, source bin, destination bin,...ect
  • Picking list - WO Multiple - Contents WT list of 1 WO, Product, pick quantity, source HU, source bin...ect

Example Standard WO Smart Form 

Prerequisites

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Print

Define Condition Types

Define Determination Procedure and assign Condition Type to Determination Procedure

Assign Determination Procedure to Warehouse and WPT

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Print > Define PPF Action Profiles

"Inactive" must not be ticked.

Create Condition record, Tcode: /SCWM/PRWO6 - Link Form with action definition

Condition is set to trigger PPF action, different form/ label can be set based on combination of WO status (open, confirmed, cancelled, blocked, in process), Activity Area, WPT and to be printed in different printer.

When WO/WT create or confirmed (depends on WO status set in condition), postprocessing framework calls the /SCWM/WME application with action definition /SCWM/WO and checks for the condition record for print action definition.

Example: Standard form /SCWM/WO_SINGLE

Example Standard form /SCWM/WO_MULTIPLE

 

 

Print Handling Unit

EWM can use this function to automatically print lists or labels according to your Customizing settings, when you work with handling units (HUs). The documents can contain information about the contents, destination, owner, product number, quantity, batch number, weight, volume and data necessary for further processing of the HU.

You can use this function in the warehouse management monitor manually perform or repeat printing for a HU.

Example scenarios to trigger HU label:

  • Creating HUs in work centers before putaway
  • Repacking HUs in work centers
  • Creating shipping labels for HUs before loading

Example Standard HU Form

Prerequisites

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Handling Units > Basics > Print

Define Condition Types

Define Determination Procedure & Assign Condition Type to Determination Procedure

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Handling Units > Basics > Print > Define PPF Action Profiles

Condition record, Tcode: /SCWM/PRHU6 - Link Form & Action Definition

Different HU label can be set based on combination of HU type, HU step (create, change, delete, manual trigger HU printing), packaging material.

When you save HUs or confirm a warehouse task, the system calls the PPF and starts an attempt to print. The decision whether to print is dependent on the condition records that are found in the schedule condition.

Example Standard form /SCWM/HU_SHPLABEL

Example Standard form /SCWM/HU_CONTENT

Print TU Loading List

In shipping and receiving, you can print the following documents for a vehicle or a transportation unit (TU) using PDF-based forms or SAP Smart Forms forms. This part will show example about TU Loading List.

In the header area of a TU or vehicle in the warehouse management monitor, you can manually print a TU-related document or vehicle-related document.

Example Standard Form

Prerequisites

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Shipping and Receiving > Message Processing > Define Action Profiles for Transportation Units

Tcode: /SCWM/SR_PRINT - Set Up Printer and Form Determination

Create a Print Profile, then assign Action definition + Printer to Print Profile

Define Access Sequence and assign Print profile + Form name 

Example Standard from /SCWM/SF_SR_TULOADLIST

Print Physical Inventory count sheet

The PI counting list includes PI counting items for the counting process. It also contains the warehouse order and PI document number as a bar code on header level.

The PI counting list contains the following information by default: Storage bin, Handling unit, Product, Product description, Batch, Quantity (for input), Unit of measure...

Form template /SCWM/PI_CNT_FIORI for landscape orientation or /SCWM/PI_CNT_FIORI_POR for portrait orientation.

Prerequisites

IMG: Extended Warehouse Management > Internal Warehouse Processes > Physical Inventory > Print

Define Condition Types

Create and Change Determination Procedure

Specify Action Profiles for Printing Physical Inv. Documents

Tcode: /SCWM/PRPI_GCM - Create Condition Records for Printing (Physical Inventory) 

Link Form & Action Definition

Print by Tcode: /SCWM/PI_PROCESS 

or Tcode: /SCWM/PRPI_DOC

Example standard form /SCWM/PI_CNT_DOC

Delivery Note print

EWM automatically prints the delivery note for an outbound delivery as soon as you have posted the goods issue. By default, EWM uses the predefined SAP Smart Form /SCWM/DLV_NOTE.

Prerequisites

IMG: Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Delivery - Warehouse Request > Actions > Change Actions and Conditions Define Conditions

Define Action Profiles and Actions

IMG: Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Outbound Delivery > Print

Define Print Profiles for Delivery Processing

Define Print Profile Determination for Goods Issue Process

Determine Form Determination for Delivery Note Printing

Tcode: /n/SCWM/DLVPPFC

Example Standard Delivery Note

Reference

Integration of PPF and the Condition Technique 

How to use PPF in EWM

SAP HELP document for EWM PPF Printing

***

Thank you for your reading. I would like to receive any comment/question regarding my post.

entry.title: Outbound process with SAP EWM: POSC, WOCR & Wave Management
entry.content:
Outbound logistics is a critical component of supply chain management, ensuring products are delivered to customers efficiently and accurately. In #SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), advanced tools like Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC), Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR), and Wave Management enable seamless execution of outbound processes. This blog provides an overview of a configured scenario to manage a complex outbound process, highlighting each component's role. 

Scenario Overview:

1. Customer Orders and Sales Order Creation:

  • Customer orders are received and processed in SAP.
  • A Sales Order is created in SAP S/4HANA based on customer requirements.
  • An Outbound Delivery is generated against the Sales Order in S/4HANA and distributed to SAP EWM as an Outbound Delivery Order (ODO).

2. Wave Management:

  • The actual EWM process begins upon the creation of the ODO.
  • In this scenario, Waves are created Automatically. A Wave groups multiple Outbound Delivery Orders
    based on specific criteria. For this case, the grouping condition is the Customer.
  • A Wave consolidates all ODOs associated with a single customer, facilitating streamlined operations.

3. Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR):

  • WOCR is used to group Warehouse Tasks into Warehouse Orders based on predefined filters and limits.
  • It also supports the automatic creation of Pick Handling Units (Pick HUs) via packing profiles.
  • In this scenario, three WOCRs are configured for different resources:
    • Human Resources
    • Trolley
    • Forklift

4. Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC):

  • POSC defines the sequential steps required to execute the outbound process.
  • In this scenario, the process includes:
  1. Picking: Pick Handling Units (HUs) are created during picking and moved to the packing work center.
  2. Packing: Products are packed by warehouse associates at the work center. Upon confirmation, an automatic Warehouse Task is created to move products to the Goods Issue (GI) staging area.
  3. Loading: Tasks are generated and confirmed to load products onto the delivery vehicle.
  4. Goods Issue: Final Goods Issue is performed, generating an Outbound Delivery Document in the system.

Upon Goods Issue, Post Processing Framework (PPF) actions are triggered to send the required information back to S/4HANA.

Configuration

This configuration focuses on the key steps, intentionally omitting basic configurations for the sake of brevity. It encompasses three critical components: Wave Management, WOCR, and POSC setup.

I.  Wave Management

As previously mentioned, this configuration involves auto wave creation using PPF Actions and PPF.

1. Maintain Wave Type
2. Maintain Wave Categories
3. Set Automatic Wave Generation for Warehouse Process Type
3.1 PPF Configuration

T.Code: SPPFCADM

Application

/SCDL/DELIVERY

Action Profile

/SCWM/PDO  01

Action Definition

/SCWM/PDO_01_WAVE

Method

/SCWM/WHR_WAVE_ASSIGN_NEW

Make sure Action Definition listed above active for AUTO Wave Generation.

3.2  Outbound Delivery Process Document Types
4. Define Condition Table
5. Define Access Sequence
6. Define Condition Types
7. Define Determination Procedure
8. Assign Procedure to Document type

9. Define Condition Maintenance Group
10. Maintain Wave Template

 
11. Maintain Wave Condition Record

II. Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR)

In the context of WOCR, specific requirements have been defined based on the handling capacities of different resources:

  • WOC1: This rule applies to human resources where the load restriction is a maximum of 2 kilograms, suitable for items that can be carried manually by hand.
  • WOC2: This rule is designated for trolley-based operations, allowing a load capacity of up to 30 kilograms, making it ideal for medium-sized items that require mechanical assistance for movement.
  • WOC3: This rule is intended for heavy-duty equipment capable of managing loads up to 500 kilograms, suitable for large or bulky items that demand specialized handling equipment.

For detailed information and additional context regarding these requirements, please refer to the introduction section provided earlier. The basic configurations, such as number ranges for Warehouse Orders and sorting rules like "PIPA - Pick Path," have already been set up in the system. Let’s focus on the key steps involved in configuring WOCR.

1. Define Filters for warehouse order creation rule

 

2. Define Limits values for the size of a warehouse order

3. Define packing profile for Warehouse order Creation

3.1 Packaging Specification

Packaging specifications are a prerequisite for the packing profile. When creating a Pick HU, the system uses these specifications to determine the appropriate packaging material.

3.2. Assign Bins to Activity Area

Virtual bins are created for each activity area in advance and assigned accordingly. This step  is crucial for the creation of Pick HUs.

3.3 Warehouse process type for pick HU

3.4 Assign WPT to warehouse as WPT HU as Res

4. Define Creation Rule for warehouse Orders

5. Define Search Sequence of creation rules for activity areas

III. Process- Oriented Storage Control (POSC)

The POSC workflow involves the following steps: Upon the release of a wave, picking warehouse tasks are automatically generated, and pick HUs are simultaneously created. These pick HUs are then moved to the designated packing work center, where items are prepared for further processing. Following packing, the items are staged appropriately and subsequently loaded for shipment.

The Warehouse Process Type (WPT) "ZPIC" has already been configured in the system, and corresponding stock removal strategies have been established in alignment with the ZPIC WPT. These configurations ensure that stock removal processes are executed efficiently and adhere to the specific requirements associated with the defined WPT.

1. External Storage process

2. Define Storage process

3. Assign Storage process step

4. Process Oriented Storage Control

Assign storage process steps to their designated locations, such as the staging step to the storage type and the packing step to the corresponding work center.

IV. Work Center

The packing process requires a dedicated work center, for which the layout "VPMX" has been utilized. The standard storage type designated for the packing work center is 8030, and a corresponding bin has been created within the 8030-storage type to facilitate smooth packing operations.

1. Define Work Center

2. Assign Storage bin to Work Center

Test case:

Let’s consider a scenario where orders are received from customers. For instance, two customers place orders for three different products separately. The business requirement is to group these orders based on customers, which is referred to as a wave.

Once the wave is created, the WOCR comes into play. The configuration is set to be released immediately, warehouse tasks are automatically generated and grouped into warehouse orders based on the available resources. In this configuration, resources are categorized as Human, Trolley, and Forklift for picking operations. Additionally, Pick HUs are created automatically during this process.

Warehouse associates perform these tasks and confirm them using RF devices. The workflow then proceeds to move the picked products to the packing area, followed by staging and loading, and finally to the Goods Issue. Each subsequent task is created automatically once the previous task is confirmed which will be executed by the POSC. For simplicity, this blog demonstrates all processes using the Monitor instead of RF. In real-world scenarios, these processes are typically executed via RF in real time.

1. Sales Order Creation (VA01)

10 Sales Orders are created with two different customers for 3 products as shown in the table below. 24-2550(5Q) represents 24 is the Customer, 2550 is the products and 5Q in parenthesis is Quantity.

Sales Order

Customer-Products

Sales Order

Customer- Products

6127

24-2550(5Q)

6126

27-2565(2Q)

6132

24-2551(4Q)

6124

27-2550(30Q)

6133

24-2565(1Q)

6125

27-2551(2Q)

6130

24-2551(10Q)

6134

27-2565(1Q)

6128

24-2550(1Q)

6129

27-2550(2Q)

2. Outbound delivery creation in S/4 (VL01N)

Against Sales Order Created in the S/4, Inbound deliveries will be created, those are listed in the table below.

Sales Order

Inbound Delivery

Sales Order

Inbound Delivery

6124

80005863

6129

80005868

6125

80005864

6130

80005869

6126

80005865

6132

80005870

6127

80005866

6133

80005871

6128

80005867

6134

80005872

All outbound deliveries have been created and successfully distributed to EWM.

3. Outbound Delivery Order in EWM (/N/SCWM/PRDO)

Let’s check whether Outbound deliveries are created and distributed to EWM. Since 10 Outbound deliveries created, searched with advanced search.

Auto Wave Creation:

Waves can be created manually using T-code /N/SCWM/WAVE, but Auto Wave Creation enabled (refer the configuration step I. 3). This process starts with the WPT, triggering PPF actions to generate waves using wave templates 101 and 102, based on condition records configured earlier (refer configuration I. 11). The system created two waves: 5039 for Business Partner 148 and 5040 for Business Partner 129. The PPF log (see image) illustrates the wave creation process as per configuration (refer configuration step I. 3.1). Let’s get into the warehouse Monitor for wider visibility.

4. Wave Release (/N/SCWM/MON)

Two waves were created immediately upon delivery distribution, and they were automatically released. The wave status is marked as "R" (Released), and the wave release method is "B" (Immediate Release), as defined in the wave template. The configuration ensures that outbound delivery orders are grouped by the Ship-to Party, with one wave created for each customer. For instance, outbound deliveries for customer 129 were grouped into a single wave, as shown in the image.

The setup ensures that waves are automatically released for picking tasks as soon as they are created. Alternatively, manual wave release is also possible through the Warehouse Monitor or T-code /N/SCWM/WAVE, depending on the configuration in the wave template.

Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR):

Next, let’s explore the steps following wave release by focusing on a single wave to understand how warehouse operations are executed. Once the wave is released, the system immediately refers to the Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR) for creating warehouse orders.

In this example, three WOCRs were configured, and each was triggered based on the defined filters and weight limits. As shown in the image, the system created the following warehouse orders:WOC1: 2 kg, WOC2: Three orders of 5 kg, 5 kg, and 25 kg and WOC3: 150 kg.

These ranges are clearly defined in the WOCR configuration, where the link between the wave and WOCR is established through the wave category specified in the WOCR filters (refer to the configuration step- II.1).

Warehouse Task creation:

After WOCR processing, the focus shifts to the Warehouse Tasks assigned under the WO. These tasks are created to move stock from the picking storage type to the packing work center, as defined in the configuration.

This process follows the POSC configuration. As shown in the image, the WPT is ZPIC, with the Storage Process and External Step both triggered as ZPIC. The POSC is activated via WOCR, as the storage process is linked in the WOCR determination (refer to configuration step II.4). Picking operations are entirely driven by stock removal strategies, which play a crucial role in the creation of warehouse tasks for picking. However, detailed steps for stock removal strategies are omitted in this blog to maintain its brevity. It’s important to note that understanding these strategies is essential for efficient picking processes in the warehouse.

Let’s confirm picking warehouse tasks…

Auto Pick HU:

Upon confirmation of the warehouse tasks, the destination bin automatically changed to "VIRTUAL_BIN1". This behavior is linked to the Pick Handling Unit (Pick HU) configuration. The system is set to automatically create Pick HUs during the picking process, as defined in the WOCR Packing Profile (refer configuration step- II.3).

Based on the packaging specifications configured in the system, the system assigned the appropriate packaging material to each task. As shown in the image, a unique Pick HU number was created for each WT, ensuring proper handling and alignment with the configuration.

Next, let’s verify whether follow-up tasks were created to move the products from the VIRTUAL_BIN1 to the Packing Work Center for packing. To identify these subsequent tasks,  filter for Open WT with the WPT set to 3070, as specified in the POSC configuration (refer to configuration step III.4).

Subsequent tasks are created based on the external step "ZPAC" with WPT 3070, as configured in the POSC. Confirming these tasks moves the products to the Packing Work Center, which is linked to storage type 8030 and an assigned storage bin (refer to configuration step IV).

After confirming these tasks, no further tasks are generated immediately, as the system awaits a completion update from the work center. Once the system receives this update, it automatically creates the next set of tasks to move the products out of the work center. To update the completion status, the transaction code /N/SCWM/PACK is used.

Packing Work Center:

The "PACK" work center is configured as the designated Packing Work Center using the relevant T-code. In this interface, SAP provides a variety of features, including creating packages, repacking, and other functionalities, to pack products according to customer requirements.

Staging:

Upon completing the process step in the Packing Work Center, the system generates additional tasks with the external step "ZSTG", as illustrated in the image below.

These tasks are created to move the products from the Packing Work Center to the Goods Issue (GI) Staging Area. Let’s proceed to confirm these tasks as well.

Loading:

Once these tasks are confirmed, another set of tasks is generated to move the products from the Staging Area to the Door, completing the loading process. This marks the conclusion of the POSC workflow.

Let’s repeat the same process for the other wave, although the detailed steps are omitted in this blog for brevity. As mentioned earlier, in real-world scenarios, these tasks are not executed in the Monitor but are performed using RF devices. Finally, let’s check the status of the outbound deliveries in the PRDO transaction.

Goods Issue:

As shown in the image, the status of all outbound deliveries has been updated to Picking Completed, Packing Completed, and Loading Completed, indicating successful execution of the POSC process. By selecting Goods Issue, the system completes the goods issue for these outbound deliveries. Once the goods issue is completed, an outbound delivery document is created, triggering PPF actions to transfer this information to the ERP system.

Outbound delivery Document:

Outbound delivery Document created and PPF actions triggered to pass information to the ERP system as expected.

Let’s check whether the information passed to S/4..

T.code : VL03N-  S/4 Display Outbound Delivery

To illustrate the process,  verifying and displaying one of the outbound deliveries in this blog.

The Goods Issue information has been successfully updated in S/4HANA, as indicated by the Goods Movement Status marked as "C" (Completed). The system also triggered the Movement Type 601 in S/4, confirming the successful transfer of goods.

Conclusion

This blog explored the seamless execution of warehouse processes using Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC) in SAP EWM. Starting with Auto Wave Creation & Release and Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR), demonstrated how tasks are efficiently grouped and executed. The flow covered picking, packing, and goods movement from the Picking Storage Type to the Packing Work Center, Goods Issue Staging Area, and finally to the Door for loading.

Key configurations, such as Pick HUs and task automation, were highlighted, along with the integration of EWM and S/4HANA, where updates like Goods Movement Status and Movement Type 601 ensure real-time synchronization. While RF execution and repetitive steps were omitted for brevity, this blog showcased SAP EWM’s ability to optimize warehouse operations efficiently.

entry.title: Product Change Logs in EWM Warehouse Monitor
entry.content:
SAP Extended Warehouse Management in SAP S/4HANA offers a feature to track and display product change logs directly within the Warehouse Monitor. This functionality enhances transparency by enabling users to monitor modifications made to warehouse products. Below is a step-by-step guide to activating and utilizing this feature.

Activating Change Document Recording:

By default, change document recording for warehouse products is not activated in the system. To enable this functionality, follow these steps:

We can activate the change document recording for the Warehouse product by following the below SPRO customizing path.

SPRO ---> SCM Extended Warehouse Management ----> Extended Warehouse Management ---->Master Data ----> Product -----> Activate Change Documents.On the activation screen, select the entry for "Product (Warehouse-Specific)" and click on "New Entries."Check both options to enable logging for changes in the "Warehouse Data" view and the "Storage Type Data" view.
Save your changes. These settings can also be transported to other systems.

Testing the Functionality:

To verify that the change document recording is active: Open transaction /SCWM/MAT1 (Maintain Warehouse Product).
Select a product, e.g., TG-100.
Make a change, such as modifying the process type determination indicator (e.g., changing from TG to RM).
Save the changes.

Viewing Product Change Logs:

Once the change document recording is active, you can view the logs using two methods:

1. Using Warehouse Monitor:

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Master Data → Product → Display Change Documents.

Open transaction /SCWM/MON.
Select the node for "Product Master Data."
Enter the product details and execute.
Navigate to "More Methods" and choose "Display Change Documents." This will show all changes made at the warehouse product level2.Using Transaction Code /SCWM/PROD_CD_READ

Execute /SCWM/PROD_CD_READ.
Enter the following details:
User: Specify the user who made changes.
Date & Time: Define the range for tracking changes.
Product: Input the product code.
Warehouse Number and Party Entitled to Dispose.
Click "Execute" to display all logged changes for the specified product and timeframe.Benefits of Product Change Logs: 

Enhanced transparency and traceability of changes made to warehouse products.
Improved control over master data modifications.
Robust auditing capabilities for compliance purposes.
This feature is a valuable addition for organizations aiming to maintain accurate records of warehouse operations and ensure accountability within their EWM systems.

 

Reference SAP notes, 3485825 - Activation & display of Warehouse Product change documents

 

Best Regards,

Kannan Chokkanathan.

entry.title: Outbound process, Picking by Warehouse order using RFUI
entry.content:
Outbound Process:

The outbound process in SAP EWM involves picking, packing, and shipping goods to fulfill customer orders. It begins with creating outbound deliveries and ends with goods issue, confirming that items have been dispatched. SAP EWM helps optimize the entire process by managing warehouse tasks efficiently.

Radio Frequency User Interface:

The Radio Frequency (RF) user interface provides warehouse workers with a mobile, handheld device to interact with the system in real-time. It enables efficient execution of tasks like picking, packing, and confirming activities with minimal manual effort. RF devices increase accuracy and speed by providing direct communication with the warehouse management system.

Picking:

Picking is the process of retrieving items from storage locations based on a customer order or warehouse request. The system can generate picking tasks and assign them to warehouse operators using RF devices, ensuring the correct quantity and items are picked. Various picking strategies (e.g., single or multi-order picking) can be configured for optimal efficiency.

Warehouse Order:

A warehouse order is a collection of tasks grouped together to optimize the warehouse worker's efficiency. It can include tasks for picking, packing, and other related activities. Warehouse orders streamline the execution process by organizing tasks into manageable units, helping to ensure faster and more accurate order fulfillment.

 

Steps to perform Outbound processes, Picking by Warehouse order using RFUI 

Step1: 

Create Standard order using the Transaction code VA01

 

 

Step2: 

Create Outbound delivery:

 

Outbound delivery was distributed to EWM

 

 

Step3:

Created Warehouse task and warehouse order

Step4:

Performing picking by WO in RFUI

 

 

Provide the warehouse order number

 

 

 

 

The picking by warehouse order is confirmed.

 

Picking completed.

 

Conclusion: 

Steps to perform Outbound processes, Picking by Warehouse order using RFUI is provided.

entry.title: SAP EWM - Multiple Auto Pick HU Creation for Single Warehouse Order
entry.content:
This blog shows demonstration of how individual Pick HU can be created for each Warehouse task in a single Warehouse Order. 

Business Scenario: For a Single Warehouse order with a multiple warehouse task, each warehouse task should have an individual Pick HU created automatically. 

Configuration:

  • Create WOCR (Warehouse Order Creation Rule)
  1. Creation of Limits: SCM EWM > EWM > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

2. Creation of Packing Profile: SCM EWM > EWM > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Packing Profile for Warehouse Order Creation

3. Creation of WO:  SCM EWM > EWM > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Creation Rules for Warehouse Orders

4. Determination of WOCR: SCM EWM > EWM > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order >Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

  • Assignment of Pick HU Procedure

SCM EWM > EWM > Master Data > Define Warehouse Number Control

Select warehouse number and assign procedure.

Master Data:

  • Creation of Packaging Specification (/N/SCWM/PACKSPEC)
  1. Click on Create button and Select correct Packaging Specification group. ￼
  2. Enter the correct Packaging material
  3. Maintain condition records with a correct condition type.
  • Assign Start/End Storage Bin for Activity Area (/N/SCWM/SEBA)

 

Testing:

  • Create Sale Order (VA01)
  • Create Outbound Delivery (VL01N)

  • Open Outbound Delivery in EWM (/N/SCWM/PRDO)

  • Create Warehouse Task/ Warehouse Order

  • Open and check warehouse order for Created Pick Hus

               3 Warehouse tasks are created under one Warehouse order.

              We can observe Automatic Pick HUs are created for each individual Warehouse task.

We can observe that the destination HUs automatically assigned to each Warehouse task. We can confirm the warehouse task and take print of each HU label and stick HU labels.

  • Confirm Warehouse Order

                   Once all the warehouse tasks confirmed, WO will be confirmed.

entry.title: Kit To Stock in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Warehouse management is at the core of efficient supply chain operations, and SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) plays a pivotal role in streamlining these processes. Among its many features, the Kit-to-Stock process stands out as a crucial mechanism for assembling products (kits) from individual components and stocking them for future use. This blog explores the purpose, process, and benefits of Kit-to-Stock in SAP EWM, with a practical example to bring the concept to life.

The Kit-to-Stock process involves assembling a final product (referred to as the Kit Header) from individual materials or parts (called Kit Components) and storing the assembled kit in the warehouse. This ensures that the product is ready for future use, whether it’s for fulfilling customer orders or internal production needs.

A Practical Example

Let’s break this down using a real-world scenario:

  • Kit Header: EV Charging Port Assembly
  • Kit Components: Charging Port, Connector Cable

In this scenario, the EV Charging Port Assembly (Kit Header) is the finished product created by combining components such as the Charging Port and Connector Cable. These components are picked from the warehouse, assembled, and stocked as a single unit.

Benefits of the Kit-to-Stock Process

  • Operational Efficiency: The process saves time by pre-assembling kits, reducing the need to handle individual components repeatedly.
  • Inventory Accuracy: Real-time tracking of kits and components ensures precise inventory management.
  • Flexibility: The process adapts to various scenarios, including bulk kit assembly for high-demand periods.

The Kit-to-Stock process in SAP EWM is a game-changer for warehouse management. By streamlining the assembly and stocking of kits, it enhances efficiency, reduces errors, and ensures readiness for customer demands or internal requirements. Whether you’re assembling EV Charging Ports or any other kits, this process offers the flexibility and accuracy modern warehouses need to stay competitive.

Let’s dive into configuration to make this scenario work.

Configuration:

1.   Define Order Type for VAS for KIT to stock


2.   Define Storage type for Work Center

Create a bin (KITS_BIN1) in Transaction /N/SCWM/LS01 and generate an activity area (KITS) for the specified storage type using transaction /N/SCWM/GENA. Additionally, perform bin sorting for the activity area using /N/SCWM/SBST. These steps have been omitted from this blog to maintain brevity.

3.   Process- Oriented Storage Control (POSC)

The Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC) in the kitting process is configured to manage both key aspects of the operation.

Inbound Process: POSC applies to the kitting header product, ensuring that products are moved from the kitting work center and put away into their final storage bin, making them available for sales.

Outbound Process: POSC is configured to manage the picking of kitting components from the storage area and transferring them to the kitting work center.

External Storage Process Steps

Storage Process- Definition

Assign Storage Process step

Process-Oriented Storage Control

4.   Work Center

A Work Center in the warehouse is a designated area for performing specific physical activities, replicated in SAP EWM to reflect the actual workflow. This setup allows the system to recognize the activities carried out at the Work Center, enabling warehouse associates to update information directly into the system.

In the context of the kitting process, this Work Center differs from a regular work center. Since the kitting process involves both inbound and outbound operations configured in the POSC, the storage type associated with this Work Center has the role “I - Work Center with Staging Area Group.”(Refer Step-2). This role is essential as it facilitates posting of the Goods Issue for consuming kitting components and the Goods Receipt for receiving the kitting header product.

Define Work Center

SAP provides a standard Work Center Layout, "KITS," which can be utilized for the Kitting Work Center. This layout will be assigned to the external step defined in the POSC configuration.

Assign bin to work center- /N/SCWM/TWORKST

5.   Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR)

The WOCR is essential for triggering POSC during the outbound process. In this scenario, the WOCR configuration also enables the automatic creation of Pick HUs. While WOCR offers a range of functionalities, this specific scenario focuses on utilizing the Packing Profile to facilitate the automatic Pick HU process.

  • Define Packing Profile for Warehouse Order Creation

For Auto Pick HU, There are more configuration to be done, listed down the steps but screenshots are omitted. if you are interested please refer the following link for detailed Configuration on WOCR
Link: https://www.stelliteworks.com/warehouse-order-creation-rule-wocr-in-sap-ewm-for-picking/

  • Packaging Specification with “0BDL- Work Order Creation” in transaction /N/SCWM/PACKSPEC.( Refer Step-11)
  • Assign Additional Attributes to Activity Area in transaction /N/SCWM/SEBA.
  • Define Warehouse Process Type for Pick HU with Internal Movement.
  • Assign pick HU WPT in Warehouse Number Control.

Define Creation Rule for Warehouse Order

Assign Outbound Storage Process which helps in triggering POSC in Outbound Process and assign Packing profile for auto pick HU.

6.   Warehouse Process Type

Warehouse Process Types need both inbound and outbound side and this also is used to trigger POSC in Inbound process.

  • Define Warehouse Process Type

Determine Warehouse Process Type

7.   Define Put away Strategies for Header Product after Kitting

Put away Strategies to put the finished product after kitting.

  • Define Storage type search sequence for put away

  • Assign Storage type to the Storage type search Sequence

  • Define Put away Control Indicator

  • Specify Storage type search sequence for Put away

8.    Stock Removal Strategies

Stock Removal Strategies to pick the components from storage area to  Kitting Work Center

  • Define Storage type search sequence for stock removal

  • Define Control Indicator for Search Sequence
  • Specify Storage type search sequence for Put away

9.   Bill Of Materials (BOM)

 10.  Packaging Specification

Pack Specification detailed configuration is skipped in this blog.

 

11.   Product Master

Header:

Components:

 

The Kit-to-Stock process is an integral part of Value-Added Services (VAS) in SAP EWM. The process begins with the creation and maintenance of VAS for Kit-to-Stock.

Maintain VAS- Kit to Stock- /N/SCWM/VAS_KTS

SAP provides the flexibility to create kits with or without a Bill of Materials (BOM)

  • With BOM: Utilizes an existing BOM already configured in the system.
  • Without BOM: Enables the addition of components directly to the header product.

In cases where BOMs are used, they are created using CS01, as shown in Step 9. The configuration involves assigning the order type, packaging specification, and the header product along with its quantity. This setup allows the creation of the VAS for Kit-to-Stock, as illustrated in Steps 1, 10, and 11 respectively.

Outbound and Inbound Deliveries in the Kitting Process

The Kit-to-Stock process involves both Outbound and Inbound deliveries

  • Outbound Delivery: Created to pick the components required for kitting from storage bins and transfer them to the kitting work center.
  • Inbound Delivery: Created in the header details of the kit to facilitate the put away of the final product back into storage bins after the kitting process.

 

Outbound & Inbound Deliveries

Outbound Delivery- /N/SCWM/PRDO

The Outbound Delivery created manually as see indicator in the image and it includes two line items representing the components required to form the kit. These line items are configured with the warehouse process type "KTSO", as per Step 6. Outbound delivery is to create picking warehouse tasks for picking the components from storage bins and transferring them to the kitting work center.

 

  • Inbound Delivery- /N/SCWM/PRDI

Inbound Delivery (Warehouse Request) is automatically created to facilitate the movement of the final product, assembled through the kitting process, to the designated storage bin. This ensures the product is ready for sale and can be efficiently shipped when an order is placed.

The Inbound Delivery contains only one line item, representing the final product. This line item is created with the warehouse process type “KTSI”, as illustrated in the image below. This process ensures seamless integration of the kitting output into the warehouse workflow, enabling quick availability for shipping.

 Creating Picking Warehouse Task for components for kitting

To create warehouse tasks for picking manually, the system selects the products from the ET10 storage type, as shown in the image below. These products are available in the bin ET10-10-14-04, which belongs to the ET10 storage type. The picking process is driven by the Storage Type Search Sequence for stock removal, configured in Step 8, ensuring that the system efficiently identifies and retrieves the required products.

Upon confirmation of the warehouse task, the products are transferred to the kitting work center bin KTS_BIN1. However, the system generated an additional warehouse task due to the configuration in Step 5, where auto pick HU was enabled. To proceed, filter the warehouse tasks using the process type “3070” and open the warehouse tasks in the monitor due to the configuration in Step-3.

Only one warehouse task was found because both products were picked in the same pick HU and moved together to KTS_BIN1. Upon confirmation, the products are transferred to the work center as shown in the below image. At the work center, associates perform the kitting process and update the system to reflect the completion of the activity.

Confirmation of VAS Order (Kit To Stock)-/N/SCWM/VASEXEC

 The products, along with their bin and VAS order details, can be viewed in this transaction. Under the Post Components section, if the HU is posted using the Post HU button, the system will perform a goods issue for those products in the outbound delivery, indicating that the products have been consumed for the kitting process.

Now Let’s pack them in new HU for processing

The system navigates to the packing work center, where the final product is packed into a new HU, as shown below. After packing, save the changes, and upon returning to the previous screen, the newly packed HU will be listed in the kitting work center bin.

When the newly packed HU containing the final product is posted by clicking the Post HU button, a Goods Receipt is recorded, indicating that the final product has been successfully created in the system.

Next, click on “Complete Process Step for HU” and save the changes. The system will automatically create a warehouse task (52273) to move the product from the work center to the designated storage bin, initiating the put away process.

A warehouse task is created to move the product from KTS_BIN1 to the E101-04-03 bin. This bin serves as the final storage location for the kit header product. Upon confirmation, the put away process will also be completed in the inbound delivery.

Let’s check the bin for final product

Finally, the kit product is available for sale with stock type F2. When an order for this product is received, it can be directly picked from the designated bin and shipped.

entry.title: Automatic Generation of Packaging item for Goods issue S4 HANA
entry.content:
Introduction:-

This blog includes configuration and master data set up to achieve automatic generation of packing items in delivery document.

Business requirement :-

When business need to have visibility of what packaging material used in deliveries as well as how many quantity of packing material has been consumed to prepare shipment. then we have to perform below stated configurations and master data setup to achieve business requirement.

 

To achieve requirement below configuration has to be set up.

Configuration

1. Define Packaging material types

SPRO--> Logistics Execution-->Shipping-->Packing-->Define Packaging Material Types for Shipping.

2. Define packaging material group for packing material.

SPRO--> Logistics Execution-->Shipping-->Packing-->Define Material Group for Packaging Materials

3. Define Allowed packing material types.

SPRO--> Logistics Execution-->Shipping-->Packing-->Define Allowed Packaging Materials

4. Define packaging material at EWM

SPRO--> SCM Extended Warehouse Management-->Extended Warehouse Management-->Cross-Process Settings-->Handling Units-->Basics-->Define Packaging Material Types for Warehousing

5. Maintain Allowed Packaging Material Types for Packing Group

SPRO--> SCM Extended Warehouse Management-->Extended Warehouse Management-->Cross-Process Settings-->Handling Units-->Basics-->Maintain Allowed Packaging Material Types for Packing Group

6. Assign number ranges for Packaging material

SPRO--> SCM Extended Warehouse Management-->Extended Warehouse Management-->Cross-Process Settings-->Handling Units-->External Identification-->Assign Number Range Intervals to Packaging Material Types

7. Activate automatic generation of packaging items.

SPRO--> SCM Extended Warehouse Management-->Extended Warehouse Management-->Goods Issue Process-->Outbound Delivery-->Automatically Generate Packaging Items.

Activate Delivery item for combination of warehouse and Delivery type.

select warehouse line and double click on Relevance of Pack materials.

Select line and click on Determination of Delivery Item Type and maintain delivery type item type and stock type

in this configuration assign packing material to EWM document type and item type OPHU and select stock type as F2.

8. For ECC delivery type activate automatic packaging indicator.

SPRO-->Logistics Execution-->Shipping-->Deliveries-->Define Delivery Types.

9. Assign packaging material  item categories to delivery type with usage of PACK.

SPRO-->Logistics Execution-->Shipping-->Deliveries-->Define Item Category Determination in Deliveries.

Master Data set up

1. Create Packaging material and assign packaging material type and group in sales general/plant data view what we have created in above configuration

2. If Packaging material is inventory managed then goods movement bin should be maintained in transaction

/SCWM/GMBIN_DET.

Test case:-

  • Create sales order by using transaction Tcode VA01.
  • Create delivery with reference to sales order using Tcode VL01N.
  • Perform Picking activity in EWM
  • During Packing select packing material EP1RA-01 to perform packing in transaction /SCWM/PACK for EWM delivery.
